Hospital Course:
-The patient was admitted to the cardiac catheterization laboratory with sudden onset chest pain associated with anterior ST-segment elevation on the electrocardiogram. The chest pain occurred during a bowel movement and he was found to be in cardiogenic shock upon arrival.
-Emergency diagnostic coronary angiography was performed, revealing severe proximal stenosis in the left anterior descending (LAD) artery. An extravasation of contrast media from the LAD artery into the pericardium was noted, with no flow in the distal LAD artery, resulting in a suspected dehiscence of the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) graft.
-Injection of contrast media discovered that the LIMA graft's flow of contrast only reached the mid-segment and stopped, while the saphenous vein grafts (SVGs) in the right coronary artery (RCA) and circumflex (LCx) artery were patent.
-After diagnostic coronary angiography, the patient experienced pulseless electrical activity cardiac arrest, but spontaneous circulation was achieved with CPR, and an intra-aortic balloon pump was inserted.
-Urgent echocardiography revealed a large global pericardial effusion with right atrial and right ventricular collapse. Emergency pericardiocentesis was performed to aspirate 700 mL of haemorrhagic fluid that contained an organized thrombus.
-Due to on-going haemodynamic compromise and non-availability of cardiac surgery on-site, it was decided to perform percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to treat the LAD artery stenosis. Following PCI, thrombolysis in myocardial infarction (TIMI) 3 flow was achieved, and no further extravasation was seen at the dehiscence site.
-Assessment of the LIMA graft showed a sluggish flow with extravasation of contrast media into the pericardium, requiring four coils (Tornado Embolisation Microcoils. Cook Medical, Bloomington, IN, USA) for embolization to halt the flow.
-The patient was then transferred to the local cardiac surgery unit and underwent emergency re-sternotomy and evacuation of the blood clots. His haemoglobin level post CABG (4 weeks prior to this admission) was measured at 100 g/L and dropped to 79 g/L following LIMA avulsion. He received 2 units of blood transfusion.
-The echocardiogram post-PCI revealed residual blood clots in the pericardial space that were difficult to extract percutaneously.
-The patient made a full recovery with no neurological sequelae and was discharged after a few days.
-Outpatient review at 3 months confirmed an excellent recovery without report of cardiovascular symptoms. Control coronary angiography was performed at 5 months, and the patient underwent successful PCI to mid and proximal LAD into the left main (LM) artery with LCx ostium optimization.
results.simplified:
Hospital Course:
The patient came to the heart catheterization lab with sudden chest pain. A heart test showed signs of a heart attack in the front of the heart. The chest pain happened during a bowel movement. When he arrived, his heart was not pumping blood well enough for the body. Doctors did an emergency test to look at his heart arteries. They found a serious narrowing in one of the main arteries of the heart. During the test, dye leaked from the artery into the sac around the heart. Blood flow to the lower part of this artery was blocked. This suggested that a blood vessel graft had torn open. Another test showed that blood was flowing only to the middle part of the graft. Blood flow to the other arteries was normal. After the artery tests, the patient's heart stopped beating but then started again on its own. Doctors used chest compressions to keep blood flowing. They also placed a small pump to help the heart. A heart ultrasound showed a large amount of blood had collected around the heart. The right side of the heart was collapsing. Doctors immediately drained 700 mL of bloody fluid from around the heart. They found a blood clot that had organized inside. The patient's heart was still not pumping well enough. Heart surgery was not available right away. Doctors decided to do a procedure to open the blocked artery from inside the blood vessel. After this, blood was flowing well again. No more dye was leaking from the torn area. Doctors looked at the blood vessel graft and saw slow blood flow. Dye was leaking into the sac around the heart. They placed four small metal coils to block blood flow and stop the leak. The patient was then moved to the heart surgery unit. Doctors opened the chest again and removed the blood clots. His blood count was 100 before the blood vessel graft surgery. It dropped to 79 after the graft was torn. He received two bags of donated blood. A heart ultrasound after the procedure showed some blood clots were still stuck around the heart. These could not be removed through the skin. The patient made a full recovery with no brain or nerve problems. He left the hospital after a few days. At a checkup three months later, he was doing very well with no heart problems. A heart artery test was done five months later. The patient had another procedure to open the blocked artery. Doctors also improved blood flow to another part of the heart.<|im_end|>

Hospital Course:
- The patient presented with continuous nonpsychotic unipolar major depressive disorder of 10 years duration and had a history of receiving adequate treatment trials of various antidepressants. However, he did not experience significant relief from symptoms despite being compliant with medications.
- Venlafaxine and mirtazapine were prescribed, but they did not provide significant improvement in the patient's condition.
- Ketamine was administered intramuscularly, which improved the patient's mood. However, he also developed manic symptoms after the third injection, which led to the discontinuation of ketamine.
- Lithium and paroxetine extended-release were then prescribed, which helped relieve the patient's manic symptoms and re-emerging depressive symptoms.

Diagnosis:
- Continuous nonpsychotic unipolar major depressive disorder.

Treatment:
- Venlafaxine and mirtazapine were prescribed, which were later discontinued.
- Ketamine was administered intramuscularly, and lithium and paroxetine were prescribed.

Outcome:
- The patient experienced relief from depressive symptoms with the initial treatment but developed manic symptoms after ketamine administration.
- The patient's manic symptoms resolved within 16-18 days of discontinuing ketamine, and treatment with lithium and paroxetine helped relieve both manic and re-emerging depressive symptoms.

Follow-up:
- The patient should continue taking lithium and paroxetine as prescribed.
- The patient should follow up with his primary care physician or mental health professional as needed.

Hospital Course:
The patient, an 86-year-old male, was admitted to our pain-clinic for chronic low back pain and left lower extremity pain for 2 years. The patient reported inadequate pain relief (<50%) and unwarranted side effects with oral analgesics. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the lumbar spine had revealed spinal stenosis, facet degenerative changes, and spondylosis. Per neurosurgery consult, no further surgery was indicated. 

Assessment and Plan:
Upon evaluation, patient was determined to have failed back surgery syndrome with left sacroiliac joint tenderness. Long-term pain relief was defined as pain relief >50% for at least 2 weeks post-injection, and at least 3 months post- Radiofrequency ablation (RFA). However, patient reported absence of any such pain relief. 

Disposition:
The patient was discharged with no further interventions or invasive procedures recommended. The patient was instructed to follow-up with his primary care physician for management of his chronic pain.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted] 
DOB: [redacted]
Sex: Female
Admission Date: [redacted]
Discharge Date: [redacted]

Diagnosis: Diffuse large B cell lymphoma

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 77-year-old woman, presented to our outpatient clinic with a two-month history of dysphasia. Both thyroid lobes were diffusely enlarged with the right lobe being slightly firmer than the left lobe. Palpable masses were found along the right cervical levels II and III. Thyroid function test was within the normal range. A flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy showed no remarkable findings. 

An ultrasound showed a larger-than-4-cm, irregular shaped, heterogeneously hypoechoic, solid mass occupying the entire right thyroid lobe with suspicious small internal calcifications. The mass directly invaded the right lateral and posterior wall of trachea, and encased the right common carotid artery. A fine needle aspiration was performed revealing suspicious findings for undifferentiated carcinoma. 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck showed a heterogeneously enhancing mass compared to adjacent muscle in the posterior portion of the right thyroid lobe, outgrowing towards the posterior wall of the trachea. Subsequent excisional biopsy of the thyroid lesion was done and it was histopathologically diagnosed as diffuse large B cell lymphoma. 

The patient received three cycles of R-CHOP chemotherapy as well as total 3,816 cGy of intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) which resulted in complete regression on the CT images four months after the initial diagnosis. 

Discharge condition:
The patient has been discharged with significant improvement in her condition.

Follow up care plan:
The appropriate follow-up care plan has been explained to the patient, and she has been advised to return to the outpatient clinic for a follow-up appointment.

Summary:
The patient presented with dysphasia and diffuse thyroid enlargement with suspicious findings of undifferentiated carcinoma. An excisional biopsy was done, and it was histopathologically diagnosed as diffuse large B cell lymphoma. She was treated with R-CHOP chemotherapy and IMRT which resulted in complete regression on the CT images four months after the initial diagnosis. The patient has been discharged with significant improvement in her condition with appropriate follow-up care plan advised.

Diagnosis: Diffuse large B cell lymphoma (a type of cancer that affects white blood cells)

Hospital Course:
The patient, a 77-year-old woman, came to our clinic with trouble swallowing that had lasted two months. Both sides of her thyroid gland were larger than normal. The right side felt slightly harder than the left side. Doctors found lumps in the neck on the right side near the voice box. Thyroid hormone levels were normal. A camera test to look inside the throat showed nothing unusual.

An ultrasound scan showed a large mass about 1.5 inches wide in the right side of the thyroid. The mass had uneven edges and looked darker than normal tissue. It had small hard spots inside. The mass had grown into the right side and back wall of the windpipe. It also wrapped around the main neck artery. A small needle test of the thyroid mass suggested it might be cancer.

A special scan of the neck showed a mass in the back of the right thyroid. The mass reached toward the back wall of the windpipe. Doctors then removed the thyroid mass and tested it. The test showed the patient had a type of cancer called diffuse large B cell lymphoma.

The patient received three rounds of cancer-fighting drugs called R-CHOP. She also had radiation therapy that delivered 3,816 units of energy to the affected area. Four months after the first diagnosis, a scan showed the cancer was completely gone.

Discharge condition:
The patient has been sent home with much better health.

Follow up care plan:
The care team explained the follow-up plan to the patient. She should come back to the clinic for a check-up visit.

Summary:
The patient came in with trouble swallowing and an enlarged thyroid gland. Tests suggested she might have cancer. Doctors removed the thyroid mass and tested it. The test showed she had a type of cancer called diffuse large B cell lymphoma. She was treated with cancer-fighting drugs and radiation therapy. Four months later, a scan showed the cancer was completely gone. The patient has been sent home with much better health. She should return for follow-up visits as planned.<|im_end|>

Discharge Summary:
Patient Name: Not disclosed

Sex: Female

Admitting Diagnosis: Acute stroke resulting in dysarthria and left-sided hemiparesis.

Hospital Course:
A 78-year-old female with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and obesity was admitted to our tertiary care hospital following an acute stroke, which resulted in dysarthria and left-sided hemiparesis. Multiple complications were encountered during the hospital course including a code blue due to aspiration pneumonia and prolonged hospitalization. After failing multiple swallow studies, a decision was made to insert a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube for long-term enteral nutrition. A fluoroscopic evaluation identified an air-distended colonic loop, which caused the procedure to be aborted. The gastrointestinal team recommended holding clopidogrel for 5 days before proceeding with endoscopic PEG placement. The procedure was successful, and the patient tolerated tube feeds without any complications. Post-procedure imaging showed that the gastrostomy tube had traversed the lateral margin of hepatic segment 3 with an adjacent hematoma measuring 2 × 2 × 4 cm. The patient tolerated the PEG tube well and was safely discharged to a nursing home for further care.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Acute stroke resulting in dysarthria and left-sided hemiparesis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity, aspiration pneumonia, and hematoma in hepatic segment 3.

Discharge Medications:
None specified.

Follow-up:
The patient expired on day 3 in the nursing home, and the cause of death remains un-established as the patient's family denied completion of an autopsy.

A 78-year-old female with type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, and obesity was admitted to our specialized hospital after a sudden stroke. The stroke caused slurred speech and weakness on the left side of the body. The patient had several problems during the hospital stay. These included a serious medical emergency due to food or liquid getting into the lungs and a longer than expected hospital stay. Several swallowing tests did not work, so doctors decided to place a feeding tube through the belly into the stomach. A special X-ray test showed a swollen loop of large intestine, which stopped the procedure. The stomach and intestine doctors suggested stopping a blood-thinning medicine for 5 days before trying the feeding tube again. The procedure was successful, and the patient was able to receive liquid nutrition through the tube without any problems. Pictures taken after the procedure showed the feeding tube had gone through the liver on the right side. There was also a small blood clot measuring about 2 by 2 by 4 centimeters near the liver. The patient handled the feeding tube well and was safely sent to a nursing home for more care.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Sudden stroke causing slurred speech and weakness on the left side of the body, type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, obesity, food or liquid getting into the lungs, and a small blood clot in the liver.

Discharge Medications:
None specified.

Hospital Course:
The 62-year-old female patient was admitted to the hospital with complaints of two episodes of sudden onset substernal chest pain, followed by right-sided, sharp diffuse abdominal pain. She was diagnosed with non-small cell lung cancer with bone metastases a year ago and was undergoing palliative chemotherapy. During her hospital stay, the patient complained of fever, chills, and vomiting episodes leading to poor intake. She was treated with intravenous fluids, a dose of IV methylprednisone, and prednisone. She had a renal biopsy that showed evidence of acute tubular injury, focal interstitial inflammation with focal mild tubulitis. The patient's elevated creatinine was believed to be secondary to pembrolizumab, which was permanently discontinued. The patient was started on docetaxel and discharged home with a new creatinine baseline around 1.8-2.0.

Hospital Course Summary: 

Admission:
A 62-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department with chest and abdominal pain. The patient had a history of non-small cell lung cancer with bone metastases, which was being treated with chemotherapy. Physical examination, laboratory tests, and diagnostic investigations were carried out, and the patient was admitted to the hospital for further care.

Hospital course:
During her hospital stay, the patient complained of fever, chills, and vomiting episodes leading to poor intake. She was given IV fluids for management of volume depletion. Her creatinine was elevated and attributed to pembrolizumab-induced renal injury. The drug was permanently discontinued, and the patient was started on docetaxel. Her renal biopsy showed evidence of acute tubular injury, focal interstitial inflammation with focal mild tubulitis.

Discharge:
Following resolution of her symptoms, the patient was discharged home with a discharge summary outlining her hospital course and new creatinine baseline around 1.8-2.0. Prophylactic medications, including sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim and pantoprazole, were prescribed, and a follow-up plan was established. The patient was advised to return to the hospital if she experienced any new symptoms or concerns.

results.original:
Hospital Course: 

A 30-year-old male presented with fever and was transferred from a local hospital in Yunnan Province. He was diagnosed with acute purulent appendicitis and underwent an appendectomy seven days prior to admission. On admission, he had a temperature of 39.5°C and a blood pressure of 108/78 mmHg. The rest of his physical examination was normal. The laboratory investigations revealed an elevated white blood cell count and an elevated procalcitonin level. The contrast-enhanced computerized tomography (CT) scan of his abdomen showed an ileocecal abscess with a size of 2.3 cm x 2.9 cm. He was treated empirically with intravenous moxifloxacin before the results of blood culture became available.

Management and Treatment:

On Day 3, gram-positive bacilli were identified in two sets of anaerobic blood cultures. The isolate was identified as E. Lenta using MALDI-TOF MS. The appropriate antibiotics were started, and intravenous meropenem was administered at a dose of 1g every 8 hours. The patient responded promptly to meropenem therapy, and percutaneous or surgical drainage of the abscess was not performed. His fever, elevated procalcitonin level, and leukocytosis resolved without surgical intervention.

Discharge Plan:

No microorganisms were identified in the follow-up blood cultures from Day 5. On Day 10, abdominal color Doppler ultrasonography was normal. After a further 6-day course of intravenous meropenem, the patient recovered and was discharged from the hospital. Follow-up appointments were scheduled to monitor his progress and ensure complete recovery.
results.simplified:
Hospital Course:

A 30-year-old male came to the hospital with a fever. He was moved from a local hospital in Yunnan Province. He was found to have a serious infection of his appendix. Seven days before coming to the hospital, he had surgery to remove his appendix. When he arrived, he had a temperature of 39.5 degrees Celsius. His blood pressure was 108 over 78. The rest of his physical exam was normal. Blood tests showed a high white blood cell count and a high level of a protein that signals infection. A special scan of his belly showed a pocket of infection near his appendix. This pocket measured about 2.3 cm by 2.9 cm. He was given an antibiotic called moxifloxacin through a vein before test results showed what type of infection he had.

Management and Treatment:

On Day 3, tests found bacteria in two blood samples taken under special conditions. The bacteria were identified as E. Lenta using a special testing method. The right antibiotics were started. The patient received a strong antibiotic called meropenem through a vein at a dose of 1g every 8 hours. The patient got better quickly with this treatment. The doctors did not need to drain the infection through a needle or surgery. His fever, high infection marker, and high white blood cell count went away without surgery.

Discharge Plan:

No bacteria were found in blood tests taken on Day 5. On Day 10, a belly scan using sound waves was normal. After 6 more days of meropenem through a vein, the patient got better and left the hospital. Follow-up visits were set up to check on his progress and make sure he fully recovers.<|im_end|>

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Gender: Male
Age: 7 years
Date of Admission: [Date]
Date of Discharge: [Date]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the clinic with a painful tumor in the lateral-middle third of the right clavicle with functional impotence of the limb. The X-ray showed a radiolucent diaphyseal lesion, which resulted in the suspicion of a pathological fracture on active ABC. The diagnostic imaging studies, including computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging, confirmed the presence of a cavitated, multiloculated lesion in the clavicle, and a scintigraphy study was compatible with cystic bone injury and bone remodelling in relation to the prior fracture. A complete resection of the cyst was performed without prior puncture biopsy, and a block resection was carried out. The entire excised cyst was removed, preserving the epiphyseal ends. The reconstruction of the resulting critical bone defect was done by a free autologous bone graft taken subperiosteally from the diaphysis of the ipsilateral fibula. The length of the acquired graft was 3 mm greater than the bone defect length to ensure its compression fit. An intramedullary titanium nail was introduced in a retrograde-anterograde manner to stabilize the graft. The surgery was uneventful, and the histopathological analysis confirmed the diagnosis of ABC, with free resection margins.

After the surgery, the patient was immobilized for three weeks by using a short posterior splint and was kept in a sling for four weeks. Active mobilization of the limb was allowed progressively, avoiding risky sports for six months. The clinical and radiographic evolution was satisfactory, and a satisfactory radiographic incorporation and consolidation of the graft were observed at five months postoperatively. The joint balance of the shoulder was completely restored six weeks after the intervention. The intramedullary nail extraction was performed 14 months after the intervention, without incidents. After 18 months of the intervention, the child presented complete mobility of the shoulder, without pain or deformities, and the complete reconstruction of the donor area of the graft could be verified. Currently, after more than two-and-a-half years of follow-up, the child maintains an optimal functional outcome, without complications or sequels at the level of the clavicle or in the donor area of the fibula, and without signs of recurrence of the lesion.

Diagnosis:
- Painful tumor in the lateral-middle third of the right clavicle with functional impotence of the limb
- Radiolucent diaphyseal lesion with insufflated and well-delimited boundaries
- Pathological fracture on active ABC

Treatment:
- Complete resection of the cyst, preserving the epiphyseal ends
- Impregnation of bone surfaces and resection bed with lauromacrogol solution to reduce the risk of local recurrence
- Reconstruction of the critical bone defect with free autologous bone graft taken subperiosteally from the diaphysis of the ipsilateral fibula
- Stabilization of the graft by means of an intramedullary titanium nail

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was admitted to our clinic with a painful tumor in the lateral-middle third of the right clavicle with functional impotence of the limb. After diagnostic imaging studies, a complete resection of the cyst was performed without prior puncture biopsy, and a block resection was carried out. Reconstruction of the critical bone defect with a free autologous bone graft taken subperiosteally from the diaphysis of the ipsilateral fibula was done. Currently, more than two-and-a-half years after the intervention, the child maintains an optimal functional outcome, without complications or sequels at the level of the clavicle or in the donor area of the fibula, and without signs of recurrence of the lesion.
results.simplified:
Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [Name]
Gender: Male
Age: 7 years
Date of Admission: [Date]
Date of Discharge: [Date]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the clinic with a painful lump in the middle part of the right collarbone. The arm was not working properly because of this. An X-ray showed a dark spot in the bone. Doctors thought this might be a broken bone with a fluid-filled growth. More detailed scans, including a CT scan and MRI, confirmed a hollow, multilobed growth in the collarbone. A bone scan showed the bone was healing and changing after the earlier break. Doctors removed the entire growth in one piece without taking a small sample first. They also removed a small piece of bone around it. The entire removed growth was taken out, but the ends of the collarbone bone were left intact. Doctors filled the gap with bone taken from the lower leg bone on the same side. The piece of bone was about 3 mm longer than needed to fit snugly. A metal rod was placed inside the bone to hold everything in place. The surgery went well with no problems. Lab tests on the removed tissue confirmed the growth was a noncancerous bone cyst. The edges of the removed tissue were free of any remaining growth.

After surgery, the patient wore a short back brace for three weeks. The arm was then kept in a sling for four weeks. The patient slowly started moving the arm again. The patient could not do risky sports for six months. Recovery went well based on checkups and scans. At five months after surgery, the new bone had joined and healed well. Six weeks after surgery, the shoulder joint moved normally again. The metal rod was removed 14 months after surgery with no problems. At 18 months after surgery, the child could move the shoulder fully. There was no pain or deformity. The area where the leg bone was taken from had healed completely. More than two and a half years after surgery, the child continues to do very well. There are no problems or lasting effects on the collarbone or the area where the leg bone was taken. The growth has not come back.

Diagnosis:
Painful lump in the middle part of the right collarbone with the arm not working properly. Dark spot in the bone with a fluid-filled growth. Pathological fracture with a noncancerous bone cyst.

Treatment:
Complete removal of the cyst, preserving the ends of the collarbone bone. Bone surfaces and area

Discharge Summary:

Hospital Course:
The 31-year-old male patient was admitted for progressive blurred vision for 3 weeks. Brain MRI revealed multiple cystic lesions in the suprasellar cistern. Lumbar puncture was performed, which showed an increased opening pressure, elevated white blood cell count, elevated protein level, and decreased glucose level. Cerebrospinal fluid cytology revealed increased eosinophils. Cryptococcus antigen tests and an Xpert-MTB assay of the cerebrospinal fluid were negative. Next-generation sequencing of the cerebrospinal fluid identified T. solium DNA sequences. Serum and CSF were positive for C. cellulosae IgG antibodies. The patient was diagnosed with basal subarachnoid NCC and was treated with albendazole, dexamethasone, and ETV. Significant improvement was observed in the patient's symptoms and CSF findings after treatment.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Basal subarachnoid NCC.

Discharge Medications:
Albendazole, Dexamethasone, and ETV were administered.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient is advised to carefully follow up with the treatment for the condition as prescribed. It is recommended to avoid exposure to any uncooked pork or contaminated water. The patient is advised to seek medical attention immediately if there are any new or worsening symptoms.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Brain and spine infection caused by a parasite.

Discharge Medications:
The patient was given three medicines: albendazole, dexamethasone, and ETV.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should follow the treatment plan exactly as told. The patient should not eat any raw pork or drink water that may be dirty. The patient should get medical help right away if any new symptoms happen or if current symptoms get worse.<|im_end|>

Hospital Course:

A 42-year-old male with a past medical history of diabetes and hypertension presented to our hospital with recurrent episodes of right limb-shaking, right facial muscles involuntary twitching and slurred speech, which were more prominent with exercise. The patient was initially diagnosed with focal motor seizures but carbamazepine failed to prevent his symptoms. Further investigations including laboratory tests, electrocardiogram, electroencephalography, and imaging studies revealed significant stenosis of the left internal carotid artery (ICA) and scans showed a large heterogeneous plaque at the beginning of the left ICA. The diagnosis of limb-shaking TIA caused by left hemisphere perfusion impairment was considered.

Treatment:

The patient was given fluid replacement therapy, antiplatelet therapy, clopidogrel and aspirin, and statins. ICA angioplasty and stent implantation were performed with excellent restoration flow. No neurological dysfunction was observed post-operation, and the procedure was successful in completely resolving his symptoms. The patient was maintained on antiplatelet therapy and statins after stenting, and no recurrences of his limb-shaking TIA and facial muscles involuntary twitch. 

Clinical courses:

The patient had at least 3 months without any symptoms, and no new TIA. He was discharged to the care of his primary physician with recommendations to continue taking anti-platelets and statins. Follow-up visits were suggested to monitor the progress of the stenosis. The patient follows up with his primary care provider, and no other issues were reported. 

Diagnosis:

Limb-shaking TIA caused by severe left internal carotid artery stenosis. 

Benefit and risk:

The benefit of the ICA angioplasty and stent implantation is that it restored blood flow in the left hemisphere and cleared the symptoms. The patient was maintained on antiplatelet therapy and statins after stenting, and no recurrences of his limb-shaking TIA and facial muscles involuntary twitch. The risks of the procedure may include long-term stent failure, stroke, infection, and bleeding, although no complications were encountered in this case. 

Follow-up:

The patient was advised to follow-up regularly with his primary physician for monitoring and management of his diabetes and hypertension. Further follow-up visits were suggested to monitor the progress of the stenosis, and to adjust the treatment plan if needed. 

Prognosis:

The angioplasty and stenting were successful in resolving the patient's symptoms. The patient had at least 3 months without any symptoms, and no new TIA. The prognosis of this patient depends on his compliance with medical management and follow-up, and his ability to manage his underlying comorbidities, diabetes, and hypertension.
results.simplified:
Hospital Course:

A 42-year-old male with a history of diabetes and high blood pressure came to our hospital. He had repeated episodes of shaking in his right arm and leg, twitching in his right face muscles, and slurred speech. These symptoms got worse with physical activity. The patient was first thought to have seizures affecting part of his body. However, a seizure medicine called carbamazepine did not stop his symptoms. More tests were done including blood tests, heart rhythm tests, brain wave tests, and imaging scans. These showed a major narrowing of the left neck artery that carries blood to the brain. Scans also showed a large buildup of fatty material at the start of this artery. Doctors believed the patient was having mini-strokes with shaking caused by poor blood flow to the left side of his brain.

Treatment:

The patient was given fluids through a vein, blood-thinning medicines called clopidogrel and aspirin, and cholesterol-lowering medicines. A procedure was done to open the narrowed artery and place a small mesh tube called a stent to keep it open. Blood flow was restored well. The patient had no brain or nerve problems after the procedure. The treatment completely stopped his symptoms. The patient continued taking blood-thinning medicine and cholesterol-lowering medicine after the stent was placed. His mini-strokes and facial twitching did not come back.

Clinical courses:

The patient had at least 3 months without any symptoms. He had no new mini-strokes. He was sent home with his regular doctor to keep taking blood-thinning medicine and cholesterol-lowering medicine. Follow-up visits were suggested to check on the narrowing in his artery. The patient sees his regular doctor and no other problems were reported.

Diagnosis:

Mini-strokes with shaking caused by serious narrowing of the left neck artery that carries blood to the brain.

Benefit and risk:

The artery opening procedure and stent placement restored blood flow to the left side of the brain and cleared up the symptoms. The patient continued taking blood-thinning medicine and cholesterol-lowering medicine after the stent was placed. His mini-strokes and facial twitching did not come back. The risks of the procedure include long-term stent failure, stroke, infection, and bleeding. However, no problems happened in this case.

Follow-up:

The patient was told to see his regular doctor often to check and manage his diabetes and high blood pressure. More follow-up visits were suggested to check on the narrowing in his artery. The care plan would be changed if needed.

Prognosis:

The artery opening

Hospital Course:

This 48-year-old Hispanic man presented with abdominal and back pain, fevers, nausea, vomiting, dysuria, postvoid dripping, and constipation. Initial exam revealed tenderness in the right costovertebral angle. CT scan showed thickened bladder wall, bilateral hydronephrosis, hepatomegaly, and perihepatic ascites. He underwent JJ stent placement and was discharged on oral augmentin.

He returned three weeks later with nausea, vomiting, poor appetite, and abdominal pain. Ultrasound showed gall bladder sludge and HIDA scan showed total retention of Tech 99 in the liver. A CT scan showed pancreatitis, hepatomegaly, esophageal, and gastric varices, intact bilateral JJ stents, and portal vein thrombosis. His ERCP showed a large antral ulcer and edematous mucosa in the duodenal bulb, and he was subsequently diagnosed with poorly differentiated carcinoma involving the duodenal mucosa and poorly differentiated gastric adenocarcinoma with signet ring cells.

Laparoscopic surgery was recommended, which was converted to an exploratory laparotomy. It revealed peritoneal carcinomatosis, ascites, and frozen ascending and descending colon. An end-ileostomy was done in view of impending small bowel obstruction. He developed bilateral deep vein thromboses after the surgery and respiratory distress, requiring reintubation. Renal function started to worsen in the face of extensive carcinomatosis and liver failure, and the patient's family elected comfort care.

Discharge summary:

This 48-year-old Hispanic man presented with abdominal and back pain, fevers, nausea, vomiting, dysuria, postvoid dripping, and constipation. He was diagnosed with poorly differentiated carcinoma involving the duodenal mucosa and poorly differentiated gastric adenocarcinoma with signet ring cells.

He underwent laparoscopic surgery that was converted to an exploratory laparotomy due to peritoneal carcinomatosis, and an end-ileostomy was done in view of impending small bowel obstruction. His renal function worsened in the face of extensive carcinomatosis and liver failure, and the patient's family elected comfort care.

Hospital Course:

This 48-year-old Hispanic man came to the hospital with belly and back pain, fevers, nausea, vomiting, pain when urinating, leaking urine after urinating, and trouble having bowel movements. The first exam showed pain on the right side of his back near the kidney. A CT scan showed a thick bladder wall, fluid buildup in both kidneys, an enlarged liver, and fluid around the liver. He had a small tube placed to drain urine from his bladder and was sent home with an antibiotic called augmentin.

He came back three weeks later with nausea, vomiting, poor appetite, and belly pain. An ultrasound showed sludge in his gallbladder, and a special scan showed a blockage in the liver. A CT scan showed swelling of the pancreas, enlarged liver, swollen veins in the food pipe and stomach, and intact tubes in the bladder, but a blood clot in the main vein to the liver. A scope test of the digestive system showed a large sore in the lower part of the small intestine and swollen tissue in the first part of the small intestine. He was then found to have two types of fast-growing cancer in the small intestine and stomach.

Surgery through small cuts was suggested but had to be changed to open surgery. It showed cancer spread throughout the belly lining, fluid buildup, and frozen parts of the large intestine. A new opening was made in the belly to allow waste to leave the body because the small intestine was about to block. After surgery, he developed blood clots in both legs and trouble breathing, needing a breathing tube again. His kidney function started to get worse as the cancer spread and the liver began to fail. The patient's family chose to focus on comfort care.

Discharge summary:

This 48-year-old Hispanic man came in with belly and back pain, fevers, nausea, vomiting, pain when urinating, leaking urine after urinating, and trouble having bowel movements. He was found to have two types of fast-growing cancer in the small intestine and stomach.

He had surgery through small cuts that had to be changed to open surgery because of cancer spread throughout the belly lining. A new opening was made in the belly to allow waste to leave the body because the small intestine was about to block. His kidney function got worse as the cancer spread and the liver began to fail. The patient's family chose to focus on comfort care.<|im_end|>

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [...]

Hospital Course: 
The patient was admitted to our hospital due to suddenly developing aconuresis and paraplegia. The patient had a two-week history of abdominal pain and weakness in the lower extremities for one day. Past medical history was unremarkable, and no family history of malignant tumors was noted. Neurological examination revealed bladder-urinary dysfunction and hypoesthesia beneath the T8 sensory dermatome. A CT scan on admission showed acute pancreatitis and acute cholecystitis. The patient underwent MRI of the abdomen and lumbar spine, which showed a swollen pancreas and gallbladder with less peripancreatic exudation and liquid collection. The MRI also showed multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes and a well-demarcated, posterolateral extradural mass between T9 and T12, with definite bony destruction. PET-CT scan revealed multifocal malignant lesions, indicating multiple metastases of malignant tumors. Gastroduodenal endoscopy showed massive involvement of the gastric body and duodenum with tumors. Histology and immunohistochemistry of gastric biopsies were suggestive of BL.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient was referred to our hospital due to a sudden onset of aconuresis and paraplegia. The patient had a two-week history of abdominal pain and weakness in the lower extremities. The patient was diagnosed with acute pancreatitis and acute cholecystitis with peripancreatic exudation and liquid collection. MRI showed multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes and a well-demarcated, posterolateral extradural mass between T9 and T12, with definite bony destruction. PET-CT scan revealed multifocal malignant lesions, indicating multiple metastases of malignant tumors. Gastric biopsies were suggestive of BL. Subsequent course was palliative care and the patient was discharged for hospice care.

results.original:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 4-month-old male intact Labrador

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with several small skin lacerations of the distal limbs, hock, and tail base. On physical examination, the dog was found to be clinically healthy with normal skeletal development. Several seroma-like swellings were noted in the regions of skin trauma (hocks, tail base). Aspirates taken from these swellings revealed moderate numbers of erythrocytes with fewer neutrophils and macrophages with proteinaceous debris. These findings were consistent with a seroma or hematoma formation, and the dog was treated with amoxycillin/clavulanic acid and firocoxib.

Two months after initial presentation, the patient was examined for ongoing seroma formation and joint abnormalities. Evidence of generalized joint hyperextensibility with a range of motion greater than 180° and skin hyperextensibility and fragility was noted. The dog had a skin extensibility test of 23.5% (normal < 14.5%). A complete blood count revealed mild anemia and serum biochemistry showed a mild hypoalbuminemia and hypoproteinemia. Other serum abnormalities included a moderate hyperphosphatemia, mild elevation in ALP, and decrease in creatinine that were all considered within normal limits for a young dog.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient was suspected to have a genetic collagen disease.

Treatment:

The patient was treated with amoxycillin/clavulanic acid and firocoxib. The owners elected to humanely euthanize the patient due to the ongoing joint hypermobility and skin lacerations.

Follow-up Plan:

None.
results.simplified:
Discharge Summary

Patient: 4-month-old male intact Labrador

Hospital Course:

The patient came in with several small cuts on the front and back legs, hock area, and tail base. During the exam, the dog was healthy with normal bone growth. Several fluid-filled bumps were found in the areas of skin injury (hocks, tail base). Samples taken from these bumps showed some red blood cells, but fewer white blood cells and cleaning cells with protein buildup. These results suggested the dog had fluid or blood collecting under the skin. The dog was treated with an antibiotic and pain medicine.

Two months after the first visit, the patient was checked again for fluid buildup and joint problems. The exam showed the joints could move too far in all directions, past 180 degrees. The skin could stretch too far and was easily torn. The dog's skin could stretch 23.5 percent more than normal, which is higher than the healthy limit of less than 14.5 percent. Blood tests showed mild low red blood cell count and low protein levels in the blood. Other blood test results included slightly high phosphate, slightly high liver enzyme, and slightly low kidney waste product. All these other results were considered normal for a young dog.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient was thought to have a genetic disease affecting the body's connective tissue.

Treatment:

The patient was treated with an antibiotic and pain medicine. The owners chose to put the patient to sleep humanely because of the extreme joint movement and ongoing skin cuts.

Follow-up Plan:

None.<|im_end|>

DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient Name: Confidential 
Age: 60 
Gender: Male 
Medical Record Number: Confidential 

Admission Date: Confidential 
Discharge Date: Confidential 
Duration of Stay: Confidential 

Admitting Diagnosis: Hypercalcaemic hyperparathyroid crisis 

Hospital Course: 

The patient was admitted to our tertiary referral center from a regional hospital with hypercalcaemic hyperparathyroid crisis. He presented with a two-week history of weakness and lethargy and a one-week history of vomiting, polyuria and polydipsia. He became confused 24 hours prior to his admission and registered 12 on the Glasgow Coma Scale. On examination, he had a left-sided mass in the anterior triangle of his neck measuring 3 × 3 cm. An ultrasonography of his neck showed a 4 × 3 cm large cyst in the left lobe of his thyroid gland. He was normotensive, with a regular pulse of 70 beats/min. 

Initial management included aggressive fluid resuscitation, cardiac monitoring and the administration of intravenous bisphosphonates. A left thyroid lobectomy and left lower parathyroidectomy were performed. Biopsy revealed a 4 cm parathyroid carcinoma within the cyst, which was fully excised. The patient remained intubated and ventilated overnight in the intensive care unit. His metabolic laboratory profile resolved quickly following the surgery. A lower respiratory tract infection delayed his initial recovery. 

The patient was discharged home 24 days after surgery on oral calcium supplementation. He has been followed up for 6 months so far without any complications or disease recurrence. 

Summary of Care: 

The patient was admitted with hypercalcaemic hyperparathyroid crisis and managed with aggressive fluid resuscitation, cardiac monitoring and the administration of intravenous bisphosphonates. Following a left thyroid lobectomy and left lower parathyroidectomy, he was discharged home on oral calcium supplementation and has been followed up for 6 months without any complications or disease recurrence. 

Disposition: Discharged. 

Instructions: 
- Continue taking oral calcium supplementation as directed 
- Follow up with your primary care physician in 2 weeks 
- In case of any emergency, call 911 or go to the nearest emergency department. 

Signed, 
Confidential 
Attending Physician.

He had been feeling weak and tired for two weeks. He had been throwing up, urinating a lot, and feeling very thirsty for one week. He became confused 24 hours before he came to the hospital. His brain function score was 12, which means he was not alert. During the exam, doctors found a lump on the left side of his neck. The lump measured about 3 by 3 centimeters. An ultrasound of his neck showed a fluid-filled sac in the left side of his thyroid gland. It measured about 4 by 3 centimeters. His blood pressure was normal. His heart rate was regular at 70 beats per minute.

First, the patient received fluids through a vein to treat the high calcium levels. His heart was watched closely. He was given medicine through a vein to lower calcium levels. Doctors removed the left side of his thyroid gland and the lower part of his parathyroid gland. A tissue sample showed a type of cancer in the parathyroid gland. The cancer was fully removed. The patient stayed in the intensive care unit overnight. He was on a breathing machine and had a tube in his throat. His body chemistry levels returned to normal quickly after surgery. A lung infection slowed his recovery.

The patient went home 24 days after surgery. He was given calcium pills to take by mouth. He has been checked on for 6 months so far. He has had no problems or return of the disease.

Summary of Care:

The patient was admitted with a serious condition where his parathyroid gland caused too much calcium in his blood. He was treated with fluids through a vein, heart monitoring, and calcium-lowering medicine. After surgery to remove the left side of his thyroid gland and lower parathyroid gland, he went home taking calcium pills. He has been checked on for 6 months with no problems or return of the disease.

Disposition: Discharged.

Instructions:
Continue taking calcium pills as directed.
Follow up with your regular doctor in 2 weeks.
In case of any emergency,

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: Hungarian viszla
Admission Date: June 2014
Discharge Date: June 2014

Reason for Hospitalization:
The patient was admitted to UCDVH with a black, necrotic, cutaneous lesion involving the umbilical region of the ventral abdomen. The dog was dull, moderately dehydrated, and icteric on presentation.

Hospital Course:
The patient exhibited several abnormal clinical findings, including ecchymoses on the ventral abdomen and ventrolateral aspect of the thorax bilaterally, thrombocytopenia, azotemia, panhypoproteinemia, hyperbilirubinemia, increased creatine kinase, and hepatic enzyme activities. Standard management for AKI was undertaken, in addition to antimicrobial therapy given the possibility of leptospirosis, whilst awaiting test results. However, the patient's condition continued to worsen despite treatment. Euthanasia was performed at the owner’s request.

Medical History:
Tick-borne disease (Babesia spp., Anaplasma spp., Ehrlichia spp. and Hepatozoon canis) polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and Leptospira microscopic agglutination test (MAT) were negative. The patient exhibited microcardia and echogenic material in the urinary bladder consistent with hemorrhage.

Summary:
The patient, a 4-year-old Hungarian viszla, presented to UCDVH with a black, necrotic, cutaneous lesion involving the umbilical region of the ventral abdomen. The patient's clinical findings included ecchymoses, thrombocytopenia, azotemia, panhypoproteinemia, hyperbilirubinemia, increased creatine kinase, and hepatic enzyme activities. Despite treatment with standard management for AKI and antimicrobial therapy, the patient's condition continued to deteriorate. The patient was euthanized at the owner's request. Histopathological examination revealed microthrombosis and necrosis of renal glomerular tufts and, multifocally, of tubular epithelia. Transmural hemorrhage was confirmed in the bladder, and focal hepatocyte necrosis and loss were noted. Retrospective evaluation of post-mortem data confirmed a diagnosis of CRGV.

Patient Name: Hungarian viszla
Admission Date: June 2014
Discharge Date: June 2014

Reason for Hospitalization:
The patient was admitted to UCDVH with a black, dead tissue, skin wound on the belly area near the belly button. The dog was tired, slightly dehydrated, and had yellowing of the skin and eyes when it arrived.

Hospital Course:
The patient had several unusual test results. These included bruising on the belly and sides of the chest on both sides, low platelet count, kidney problems, very low protein levels in the blood, high bilirubin levels, high muscle enzyme levels, and high liver enzyme levels. Standard treatment for sudden kidney failure was given. Antibiotics were also given because of a possible leptospirosis infection. However, the patient kept getting worse even with treatment. The owner asked for the patient to be put to sleep.

Medical History:
Tests for tick-borne diseases (Babesia, Anaplasma, Ehrlichia, and Hepatozoon canis) were negative. A blood test for leptospirosis was also negative. The patient had a very small heart and blood in the bladder.

Summary:
The patient, a 4-year-old Hungarian viszla, came to UCDVH with a black, dead tissue, skin wound on the belly area near the belly button. The patient had bruising, low platelet count, kidney problems, very low protein levels in the blood, high bilirubin levels, high muscle enzyme levels, and high liver enzyme levels. Even with standard treatment for sudden kidney failure and antibiotics, the patient kept getting worse. The owner asked for the patient to be put to sleep. A tissue exam showed small blood clots and tissue death in the kidney filters. Some kidney tube cells had also died. There was bleeding through the wall of the bladder. Some liver cells had also died. Looking back at findings after death confirmed a diagnosis of CRGV, a rare disease that causes blood clots in small blood vessels.<|im_end|>

Patient Name: [Patient Name]
Gender: Male
Age: 83 years
Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the emergency room with fever and confusion, and was diagnosed with infective endocarditis (IE) by pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA), complicated with cerebral emboli, meningitis, and endophtalmia.
The initial work-up revealed leukocytosis, anemia, thrombocytopenia, raised inflammatory markers, and isocoagulant status.
Imaging studies including non-enhanced brain CT and MRI showed evidence of multiple ischemic cerebral lesions and sites of localized subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) and transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) showed a mobile tissular lesion on a prior endovascular occlusion site of the left atrial appendage (LAA) corresponded to septic vegetation, mitral valve prolapse, and calcified leaflets.
The patient underwent surgery for removal of LAAO and the native mitral valve apparatus, and a mitral biological prosthesis was implanted.
The patient died three days later due to refractory cardiogenic shock.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Infective endocarditis (IE) by pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA), complicated with cerebral emboli, meningitis, and endophtalmia.

Disposition:
The patient died during the hospitalization.

Follow-up Instructions:
None.

Serious heart infection caused by a germ called pseudomonas aeruginosa, with spread to the brain, the brain lining, and the eye.

The patient died during the hospital stay.

None.<|im_end|>

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A
Medical Record Number: N/A
Date of Admission: N/A
Date of Discharge: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient was referred to our pain clinic due to an 8-day history of postural headache, which occurred following acupuncture treatment to manage chronic lower back pain caused by postlaminectomy syndrome. The patient had undergone L4-5 laminectomy 7 years prior. The physical and neurological examinations were normal. The patient was diagnosed with a postdural puncture headache (PDPH) and was placed on bed rest. An epidural blood patch (EBP) was performed, which failed to relieve the symptoms. A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was unremarkable, while an MRI myelogram showed an abnormal fluid signal intensity in the left lumbar area, along with the left paraspinal muscle, and soft tissues at the L3-4-5 level, probably due to CSF leakage.

After conservative care, which included bed rest, hydration, and taking acetaminophen, the patient's postural headaches persisted. A repeat fluoroscopically guided interlaminar EBP was performed with minor relief. A transforaminal EBP at the L3-4 and L4-5 levels on the left side was performed. After the transforaminal EBP, the patient's headache and other symptoms subsided without complications.

At the 2-week follow-up, the patient reported being headache-free, and the activities had returned to normal.

Diagnosis:

Postdural puncture headache (PDPH)

Treatment:

Conservative care, including bed rest, hydration, and acetaminophen, was recommended. Epidural blood patches were performed, including an interlaminar approach using autologous blood at the L2-3 level with 15ml, and a repeat of this procedure using the same volume of blood at the L4-5 level. Finally, a transforaminal EBP with 3ml autologous blood was given at each level at L3-4 and L4-5 on the left side.

Follow-up Care:

The patient was discharged after 4 days. At the 2-week follow-up, the patient reported being headache-free, and the activities had returned to normal. The patient was advised to follow-up with their primary care physician if there were any changes in the symptoms.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: N/A

Hospital Course:

The patient came to our pain clinic for headaches that started 8 days ago. These headaches happened when standing or sitting up. The headaches began after a needle treatment for long-term lower back pain. The patient had back surgery 7 years ago to remove part of the spine bones. The physical exam and nerve tests were normal. The patient was told they had a headache from the needle going into the spine. The patient was told to rest in bed. A procedure was done to put blood into the spine area to stop the headache. This first procedure did not help. A special scan of the brain showed nothing wrong. Another scan with dye showed fluid leaking from the spine into the lower back area. This was likely causing the headaches.

After rest, fluids, and pain medicine, the headaches still did not go away. A second blood procedure was done using X-ray guidance. This helped a little. A third blood procedure was done through the side of the spine at two levels on the left side. After this, the headaches and other symptoms went away without problems.

At the 2-week checkup, the patient said the headaches were gone. The patient was able to do normal activities again.

Diagnosis:

Headache from the needle going into the spine

Treatment:

Resting in bed, drinking plenty of fluids, and taking pain medicine were first tried. Blood procedures were done to stop the headaches. First, blood was placed between the spine bones at one level. Then, the same amount of blood was placed again at another level. Finally, blood was placed on each side of the spine at two levels to reach the nerves.

Follow-up Care:

The patient left the hospital after 4 days. At the 2-week checkup, the patient said the headaches were gone. The patient was able to do normal activities again. The patient was told to see their regular doctor if the symptoms came back.<|im_end|>

Hospital Course:
This 7-month-old boy was admitted to our hospital for treatment of respiratory syncytial viral pneumonia, with a history of tracheoesophageal fistula and esophageal atresia surgery performed at 1 day of age. The patient showed recurrent cough and sputum, and was afebrile. The chest radiograph showed increased peribronchial infiltration and air bronchogram. Due to the risk of aspiration pneumonia, oral feeding was discontinued and the patient was given parenteral nutrition via the peripheral venous route.

Extravasation Event:
On day 6 of admission, while the patient was receiving total parenteral nutrition, a 24-gauge intravenous catheter was newly inserted into the dorsal cephalic vein at the dorsum of the patient's right hand. The patient showed signs of irritability, and the obstruction alarm of the infusion pump was triggered. Without any alarm sign, approximately 9 hours after the repositioning of the intravenous catheter, the patient's right hand and arm appeared severely swollen, and capillary refill was absent. There were several blisters observed under the catheter, and an emergency fasciotomy was performed under the suspicion of compartment syndrome.

Treatment and Care:
The patient received orthopedic surgery and fascia suturing, followed by irrigation of the area several times with saline solution. Delayed primary skin closure was performed 8 days after the fasciotomy. A skin graft was scheduled for treatment of the skin necrosis after debriding the necrotic tissue with a moist dressing. Rehabilitation treatment consisting of wrist articulation exercises was started due to the high chance of wrist joint contracture. Skin graft surgery was delayed due to pneumonia, which was diagnosed during the patient's regular wound dressing and rehabilitation treatment. It was performed 4 months after the extravasation event. At the time of this report, skin engraftment had been successfully completed, and the patient was receiving continued rehabilitation treatment.
results.simplified:
Hospital Course:
This 7-month-old boy came to the hospital for treatment of a lung infection caused by a common virus. He was born with a hole between his windpipe and food pipe, which required surgery at 1 day old. The patient had a cough and was bringing up mucus, but did not have a fever. A chest x-ray showed swelling around the airways and air trapped in the lungs. Because food could go into the lungs, the patient could not eat by mouth. Instead, he received nutrition through a tube in a vein in his arm.

Extravasation Event:
On day 6 in the hospital, the patient was getting nutrition through a tube in his vein. A new tube was placed in a vein in the back of his right hand. The patient became fussy, and the feeding pump showed a problem. About 9 hours later, the patient's right hand and arm became very swollen. Blood flow to the hand was blocked. Several blisters formed under the tube. Doctors did emergency surgery to cut open the tissue and release pressure.

Treatment and Care:
The patient had bone and tissue surgery to repair the area. The wound was cleaned several times with salt water. The wound was closed 8 days later. The skin over the area died, so doctors planned to cover it with a skin graft. They removed the dead skin and covered it with a wet bandage. The patient started exercises to move his wrist because it could become stiff. The skin graft surgery was delayed because the patient got a lung infection. This was found during regular wound care and exercises. The surgery was done 4 months after the tube problem. At this time, the new skin had grown well. The patient was still getting exercises to help his hand.<|im_end|>

Hospital Course Summary:

Admission: 
A 65-year-old female with a history of Hashimoto's disease was admitted to the hospital for malaise and nausea of one-week duration without preceding infection. She had no psychiatric history. She presented with low serum levels of sodium on laboratory findings and inverted T waves on electrocardiogram (ECG).
 
Course: 
After admission, the patient's consciousness levels improved until she presented with paranoia and visual hallucinations four days later. She was delirious and was consulted by psychiatrists, who prescribed quetiapine for her delirium. Furthermore, on the same day, she complained of nausea and was pale and sweating. She was thought to be in a state of acute circulatory failure, and urgent coronary catherization was performed for possible percutaneous intervention. She was diagnosed with Takotsubo cardiomyopathy. Administration of hydrocortisone was initiated, and the hyponatremia and delirium improved dramatically in 3 days. An echocardiogram showed the left ventricular wall motion abnormalities including apical segment to be also markedly improved by 10 days after steroid administration.

Diagnosis: 
Endocrine studies showed decreases in serum cortisol and ACTH and reduced urinary secretion of free cortisol. It was found that the patient was suffering from isolated ACTH deficiency. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed no pituitary lesions or adjacent lesions.

Discharge: 
The patient was discharged in stable condition and instructed to continue taking oral hydrocortisone. She was advised to follow up with her primary care physician and endocrinologist to manage her isolated ACTH deficiency.
results.simplified:
Hospital Stay Summary:

Admission:
A 65-year-old woman with a history of Hashimoto's disease was admitted to the hospital. She had been feeling unwell and nauseous for one week with no signs of infection. She had no history of mental health problems. Her blood tests showed low sodium levels and an abnormal heart rhythm on her heart tracing.

Course:
After being admitted, the patient became more alert. Four days later, she started seeing things that were not there and believing things that were not true. She was confused and doctors who treat mental health were called in. They gave her a medicine called quetiapine to help with her confusion. On the same day, she said she felt sick to her stomach and looked pale and sweaty. Doctors thought her body was not getting enough blood flow to her organs. They did an emergency procedure to look at her heart arteries. They found she had a condition where the heart muscle becomes weak and swollen. She was given a steroid medicine called hydrocortisone. Her low sodium and confusion got much better in 3 days. A heart ultrasound showed her heart was working better by 10 days after starting the steroid.

Diagnosis:
Tests showed her body was not making enough of a stress hormone called cortisol. Her body was also not making enough of a hormone that tells the brain to make cortisol. Doctors found that the patient had a problem making one of these hormones but nothing was wrong with her brain or pituitary gland. A brain scan showed no problems with the pituitary gland or nearby areas.

Discharge:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition. She was told to keep taking hydrocortisone pills by mouth. She was told to see her regular doctor and a hormone specialist to manage her hormone problem.<|im_end|>

The patient, a 34-year-old woman, was admitted to the Hematology Department of Tor Vergata University Hospital in Rome in [Date of Admission] with an IgA kappa multiple myeloma (MM) (DS stage IIA; ISS-1). BM aspirate revealed 50% infiltration by mature plasma cells. The patient was started on PAD chemotherapy regimen, and colchicine (1.5 g/die) was given for the treatment of the Sweet's syndrome.
The patient underwent PBSC mobilization with cyclophosphamide, and received high-dose melphalan (MEL200) with PBSC transplantation in February 2008, achieving a very good partial response (VGPR). The subsequent MM relapses were also accompanied by the occurrence of Sweet's Syndrome which resolved with colchicine and appropriate MM therapy.

Discharge diagnoses:
1. MM
2. Malignancy-associated Sweet's syndrome

The patient was discharged to [Disposition] in stable condition with instructions to follow up with the Hematology Department of Tor Vergata University Hospital in Rome.

results.original:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 20-year-old male with chronic apical periodontitis and cystic changes in the mandibular central incisors.

Hospital course:
The patient presented with pain and labial swelling in the lower anterior region for two weeks. Clinical examination revealed discolored mandibular central incisors with labial swelling and a sinus opening seen on the labial aspect of the mandibular right central incisor. Radiographic examination revealed a dumbbell-shaped periapical radiolucency associated with 31, 41. A combined approach of orthograde endodontic treatment for 31, 41 followed by periapical surgery was planned. 
The patient was administered local anesthesia, and the access cavity was done using a number 2 high-speed round diamond bur. The cleaning and shaping of the root canals were performed by hand instruments with step-back technique up to 45 ISO size K-file with alternate irrigation of 3% sodium hypochlorite solution and saline. Calcium hydroxide paste was given as an intracanal medicament for one week. The patient was prescribed antibiotics and analgesics to manage pain and swelling.
One week later, the patient was asymptomatic, obturation was done with cold lateral compaction technique, and the access cavity was restored with composite resin. A complete blood picture and coagulation studies report were normal. The general health condition of the patient was good.
Under local anesthesia, a full thickness mucoperiosteal flap was elevated. The granulation tissue was curetted, and the roots were resected and retrograde filling was done with mineral trioxide aggregate. Bone graft was placed due to the large extension of the defect. The mucoperiosteal flap was sutured in place, and the patient was periodically reviewed after 3 months, 6 months, and one year. At one-year follow-up, a radiograph was taken in relation to mandibular central incisors, which confirmed the satisfactory healing of the periapical lesion.
The patient was asymptomatic during the one-year follow-up. The final diagnosis was chronic apical periodontitis with cystic changes of mandibular central incisors and periapical periodontitis of tooth 31, 41. The recommended treatment plan was successful, and the patient was discharged in good health.
results.simplified:
Discharge Summary:

Patient: 20-year-old male with long-term gum infection at the root tips and fluid-filled sacs in the lower front teeth.

Hospital course:
The patient came in with pain and swelling on the lower front part of the mouth for two weeks. The exam showed discolored lower front teeth with swelling and a small opening on the gum side of the lower right front tooth. X-rays showed a dark area shaped like a dumbbell at the root tips of teeth 31 and 41. The care team planned to treat the teeth from inside the tooth first, then do surgery to remove the infected tissue.
The patient was given numbing medicine, and the dentist made an opening in the tooth using a spinning drill. The inside of the teeth was cleaned and shaped using hand tools and a gentle rinsing solution. A paste to help healing was placed inside the teeth for one week. The patient was given medicine to fight infection and reduce pain and swelling.
One week later, the patient had no more pain, and the inside of the teeth was filled with a rubber-like material. The opening was sealed with tooth-colored filling. Blood tests and clotting tests came back normal. The patient was in good overall health.
Under numbing medicine, the gum tissue was lifted back to expose the bone. The infected tissue was scraped away, and the roots were cut back and sealed with a special material to promote healing. Bone graft material was placed because the area of damage was large. The gum tissue was stitched back in place. The patient came back for checkups at 3 months, 6 months, and one year. At the one-year checkup, an x-ray showed the area had healed well.
The patient had no symptoms during the one-year follow-up. The final diagnosis was long-term gum infection at the root tips with fluid-filled sacs in the lower front teeth and gum infection around teeth 31 and 41. The treatment plan worked well, and the patient was sent home in good health.<|im_end|>

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: _______

Gender: Female

Age: 56 years

Admission Date: _______

Discharge Date: _______

Diagnosis: Primary Osteoarthritis of the Left Knee 

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 56-year-old female with a body mass index of 23.2, was admitted to our facility as a diagnosed case of primary osteoarthritis of the left knee. The osteoarthritis of the left knee was evaluated as grade 3 based on the Kellgren-Lawrence Scale. The patient had received an intra-articular injection of high molecular weight hyaluronic acid 2 years back which transiently improved pain symptoms. Significant improvement was not noted in the pain symptoms despite the administration of 4 injections of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) a month apart each. The patient also performed prescribed exercises that included strengthening of quadriceps and hamstrings, and stretching of the hamstrings and took precautionary measures in the activities of daily living which provided little relief in symptoms. An ultrasound of the knee joint was done to look for structural changes, and suitable changes for treatment were recommended. 

Treatment:

In view of inadequate response after 8 months of the last PRP injection, intra-articular injection of adalimumab was planned, and 10 mg of Adalimumab was administered under ultrasonography-guidance. The procedure was uneventful, and the patient did not have any transient discomfort or complications following injection adalimumab. The patient was put on Tablet Paracetamol 650 mg (as per need) as a rescue medication, and post adalimumab injection pill count was on an average 2 tablets per week. 

Outcome:

The patient reported noticeable reduction in pain beginning approximately 2 weeks from the date of injection. The 11-point visual analog scale (VAS) for pain intensity was used to evaluate the patient. VAS reduced from 8 (pre-treatment) to 3 at the 1 month follow-up, remained at 3 at the 3 months follow-up and became 4 at the 6 months follow-up. Ultrasonographic examination was repeated at the end of 12 weeks and 6 months, and pre- and post-intervention ultrasonographic findings did not show any change despite significant improvement in symptoms. The patient's knee injury & osteoarthritis outcome score (KOOS) improved from 32.20 (pre-treatment) to 61.20 at the 1 month follow-up, 60.90 at the 3 months follow-up and 57.50 at the 6 months follow-up. The patient was discharged and is under routine care in our outpatient department, and has not complained of any deterioration in symptoms. A formal evaluation of VAS, KOOS, and ultrasound examination is scheduled after 6 months. 

Recommendation:

The patient has been advised to follow the preventive measures to avoid any pain, inflammation, or stiffness in the joint. The patient has also been advised to follow-up with the treating physician regularly to avoid any complications or further injury.

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with a complaint of right flank pain for 1 year. Upon examination, his vital signs were stable, and the abdomen was soft, nontender, and nondistended, with no organomegaly or palpable lump noted.

Routine blood investigations and urinary metanephrine levels were within normal limits, and the urine examination showed the presence of pus cells, with culture sensitivity being sterile after 24 hours of aerobic incubation.

Contrast computed tomographic scan revealed a large retroperitoneal mass lesion in the region of the right adrenal gland with peripheral enhancement and central necrosis and punctate calcifications with washout of contrast. Contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging of the abdomen was suggestive of a large heterogeneous mass with central necrosis in the right suprarenal region, with peripheral enhancement.

Right adrenalectomy was done, and postoperative period was uneventful. Histopathological examination of the specimen confirmed a diagnosis of right adrenal cavernous hemangioma.

Follow-up:

The patient has recovered well post-surgery and will be advised to make routine appointments with his general practitioner. Further follow-up investigations are not necessary, and no medications have been prescribed upon discharge.

The patient’s symptoms of low back pain and radicular pain improved after the surgery. The patient could walk for more than 10 minutes with the use of a cane 1 year after the surgery. The patient was discharged in stable condition with recommendations for follow-up visits as prescribed by the surgical team.

Discharge Summary

Patient Name: [Redacted]
Date of Admission: [Redacted]
Date of Discharge: [Redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 68-year-old Japanese man, underwent resection surgery for a leiomyosarcoma at the mediastinum. Due to dissemination in the pleura, the patient received radiation therapy and a second operation. Ten months post-surgery, the patient developed pain and numbness in the left upper limb, and an enhanced solid lesion and a non-enhanced cystic lesion were observed in the apex of the left lung using contrast enhanced CT scan. Ablation using radiofrequency ablation (RFA) was performed after radiation and chemotherapy were deemed ineffective, and surgery was thought to carry a high risk of bleeding. The patient underwent RFA with a LeVeen electrode, with retractable hooks opened for ablation. A total of three ablations were performed, with the output started at 20 W and increased by 10 W every 2 minutes in each session. The procedure was performed under local anesthesia to monitor the appearance of sharp pain of the left upper limb. A maximum output of 80 W and total ablation time of 57 minutes and 14 seconds were achieved. Contrast enhanced CT performed two days after RFA showed that the enhanced solid lesion in the tumor was smaller in size compared to pre-RFA CT. The patient's pain disappeared for approximately 3 months, accompanied by an improvement in motor function disorder. However, the pain of the left upper limb reappeared due to growth of the tumor, three months after RFA.

Condition at Discharge:

The patient's pain had reappeared, and thus, he was discharged from the hospital with instructions to follow up with his healthcare provider.

Instructions:

The patient is advised to follow up with his healthcare provider regarding his condition.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: V3, Moroccan man, age 45

Admission Date: [Not specified]

Discharge Date: [Not specified]

Admission Diagnosis:

The patient presented with progressive cognitive and gait disturbances, which were attributed to cerebellar ataxia, myoclonic tremor, dysarthria, dysexecutive syndrome and a pseudobulbar affect.

Admission History:

V3 was diagnosed with insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus at the age of 8. He also had a family history of diabetes mellitus and diabetic retinopathy, with his father developing late-onset type 2 diabetes mellitus and his older sister developing insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus at the age of 8 years old with severe diabetic retinopathy. His sister suffered from a cerebrovascular accident at the age of 40, followed by an acute lower limb peripheral arterial occlusion complicated by a severe infection which ultimately led to her passing away. V3's progressive visual impairment from the age of 33 was initially ascribed to diabetic retinopathy. He had two incidents of hypoglycaemic coma at the age of 38 and 41 years old, which were followed by neurological impairment with progressive cognitive disturbances, apathy, gait instability, urinary incontinence and dysphagia.

Hospital Course:

Upon admission, neurological examination demonstrated the presence of cerebellar ataxia, myoclonic tremor, dysarthria, dysexecutive syndrome and a pseudobulbar affect, which were found to be attributed to generalised cortical and cerebellar atrophy seen on brain MRI with bilateral optic nerve atrophy. Though V3 had a history of diabetes mellitus, he did not present with features of diabetes insipidus on MRI. Ophthalmological examination with fundoscopy and optical coherence tomography (OCT) revealed bilateral optic atrophy without evidence of diabetic retinopathy. The patient underwent an abdominal CT scan that revealed several cysts in the right kidney with no involvement of the left kidney, liver, spleen and pancreas. Renal function was preserved with normal blood levels of creatinine and urea nitrogen with no associated urinary tract abnormalities.

Course at Discharge:

V3 has been assessed by a neurologist who has advised on the progression of symptoms, non-operative management and good communication between clinical teams. No follow-up appointments have been scheduled at this time.

Discharge Medications:

[Not specified] 

Follow-Up Appointments:

No follow-up appointments have been scheduled at this time.

Discharge Diagnosis:

The patient has progressive cognitive and gait disturbances attributed to generalised cortical and cerebellar atrophy with bilateral optic nerve atrophy, likely owing to his underlying insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus.
