Name: [Patient Name]
Age: 65 years
Gender: Male
Admission Date: [Admission Date]
Discharge Date: [Discharge Date]
Reason for Admission: Painful growth at right lower back tooth region

HOSPITAL COURSE:

The patient was admitted to our hospital with a chief complaint of painful growth in the right lower back tooth region. The patient reported having a history of habitual chewing of pan for the past fifteen years at a rate of 3-5 packets per day. On examination, a 4 × 3 cm, tender, hard ulceroproliferative growth was found, arising from the edentulous ridge of 45,46 extending into the alveolus and right buccal mucosa. The edge of the growth was rolled out and had an indurated base, which was confirmed on palpation. A fixed lymph node measuring 3 cm × 2 cm was also palpable at the right submandibular region.

A provisional diagnosis of malignant ulcer involving the right alveolus and buccal region in relation to 45,46 was made. Further investigations, including a CECT (Computed Tomography with Contrast) scan, were conducted, which showed a malignant mass lesion with lymph node secondaries. A biopsy confirmed the presence of well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (SCC). Based on TNM staging, the cancer was classified as IVA-T4a N1 M0.

TREATMENT:

The patient was advised to undergo surgical intervention to remove the cancerous growth, but he declined the treatment. Due to the stage of the cancer and the patient's unwillingness to undergo surgery, he was referred to palliative care for pain relief and symptom control.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:

Well-differentiated SCC of the right alveolus and buccal region in relation to 45,46 with lymph node secondaries.

RECOMMENDATIONS:

The patient has been advised to regularly follow up with his oncologist for the management of his pain and symptoms. He has also been advised to abstain from the use of pan or any other tobacco-related products. The patient has been educated on the importance of a healthy diet and lifestyle in the management of cancer.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:

None

FOLLOW-UP:

The patient is scheduled for a follow-up appointment with his oncologist in two weeks. The patient has been advised to contact the hospital in case of any new or worsening symptoms. A copy of his medical records has been shared with the oncologist for further management.

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 70-year-old female with congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and end-stage renal disease, was admitted to the general surgery clinic with a radiographic finding of a CCF. During workup for upper gastrointestinal bleeding, a CT enterography incidentally revealed a fistulous connection between her gallbladder and the proximal transverse colon. She reported right upper back pain that was unrelated to meals in the clinic. She had no abdominal tenderness and her liver function tests were within normal limits.

Medical Management:

The patient was hospitalized for surgical repair of the CCF with cholecystectomy, using the da Vinci Xi robotic system. The evaluation revealed an inflammatory connection between the gallbladder fundus and the proximal transverse colon, which was successfully separated. The gallbladder was dissected in a top-down fashion and a critical view of safety was obtained. Locking robotic clips were applied to the cystic duct and cystic artery, and the structures were transected. The colotomy was closed in a two-layer running fashion.

Outcome:

The patient recovered uneventfully and was discharged on the second postoperative day. Histopathological examination of the specimen revealed no evidence of malignancy, and the patient was seen in the clinic without postoperative complaints.

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 70-year-old woman with heart failure, irregular heartbeat, and kidneys that no longer work. She came to the surgery clinic because a scan showed an abnormal connection between her gallbladder and part of her large intestine. During tests for bleeding in her upper digestive system, doctors found this problem by chance. She said she had pain in her upper back on the right side. This pain was not related to eating. She had no belly pain when touched and her liver tests came back normal.

Medical Management:

The patient stayed in the hospital to have surgery to fix the abnormal connection. The surgeon also removed her gallbladder. The surgery was done using a robot system called da Vinci Xi. During the exam, doctors found swelling and irritation where the gallbladder meets the large intestine. They were able to separate these two areas. The gallbladder was cut from the top down. The surgeon took extra steps to make sure no important blood vessels were damaged. Small clips were placed on the tube that carries bile and the blood vessel to the gallbladder. These were then cut. The opening in the intestine was closed in two layers.

Outcome:

The patient healed well without any problems. She left the hospital two days after surgery. Lab tests on the removed tissue showed no cancer. At her follow-up visit, she had no problems from the surgery.<|im_end|>

HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:

Admission Date: [DATE]
Discharge Date: [DATE]

History and Examination:
A 54-year-old man with a history of high blood pressure and smoking presented with 9 months of epigastric pain, decreased appetite, and significant weight loss. His family history was notable for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma in his maternal aunt and breast cancer in another aunt. Physical examination was significant for an ill-defined hypoechogenic lobular area located between the head and body of the pancreas on endoscopic ultrasound. There was also a nodule at the gastroesophageal junction and a mass in the pancreas encasing the superior mesenteric artery.

Diagnostic Results:
His hepatic panel showed elevated levels of alanine transaminase and alkaline phosphatase. Carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA 19-9) was also elevated. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy resulted in the discovery of a nodule at the gastroesophageal junction. Pathology reports confirmed adenocarcinoma with KRAS amplification and NOTCH1 E905* and TP53 C135Y genomic alterations. In addition, an endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) from the GEJ nodule was also performed.

Treatment and Outcome:
The patient was diagnosed with a double primary of the pancreas and the esophagus. The patient had received 10 cycles of FOLFIRINOX with good response. At the six-month follow-up, he was doing well. 

Summary:
A 54-year-old man with a history of hypertension and smoking presented with severe and prolonged epigastric pain and significant weight loss. Diagnostic tests revealed a mass in the pancreas, a nodule at the gastroesophageal junction with adenocarcinoma with KRAS amplification and NOTCH1 E905* and TP53 C135Y genomic alterations, and an ill-defined hypoechogenic lobular area located between the head and body of the pancreas. The patient had received 10 cycles of FOLFIRINOX with a good response and was doing well at his six-month follow-up.

History and Examination:
A 54-year-old man with a history of high blood pressure and smoking came in with 9 months of upper belly pain, loss of appetite, and major weight loss. His family history included pancreatic cancer in his maternal aunt and breast cancer in another aunt. An ultrasound test using a small camera down the throat showed an unclear dark spot between the main parts of the pancreas. There was also a bump at the junction where the food pipe meets the stomach and a growth in the pancreas wrapped around a major blood vessel.

Blood tests showed high levels of liver enzymes called ALT and ALP. A marker called CA 19-9 that can signal cancer was also high. A scope test to look inside the food pipe and stomach found a bump at the junction where the food pipe meets the stomach. Tissue samples confirmed cancer with specific gene changes that can affect how the cancer grows. A small piece of tissue was also removed from the bump for testing.

Treatment and Outcome:
The patient was found to have cancer in two places: the pancreas and the food pipe. The patient had received 10 rounds of a chemotherapy drug combination called FOLFIRINOX and responded well. At the six-month checkup, he was doing well.

Summary:
A 54-year-old man with a history of high blood pressure and smoking came in with severe and long-lasting upper belly pain and major weight loss. Patient Name: N/A
Gender: Male
Age: 53 years
Hospital Admission Date: N/A

HOSPITAL COURSE:
The patient was admitted to the hospital due to ocular toxoplasmosis in his left eye with signs of posterior uveitis. The patient had a history of ocular toxoplasmosis and IgG positive antibodies for Tg. The patient was previously treated with several medications, which resulted in complete retinochoroiditis improvement. However, the anterior chamber cellularity (Tyndall 3 +) persisted, and new findings such as multiple areas of superficial circular atrophy in 360º, posterior iris synechiae, and ocular hypertension were observed. Viral origin due to the presence of IA zones and hypertensive anterior uveitis, was suspected. Therefore, Acyclovir and corticosteroids were initiated. The patient later presented cortical opacities and posterior subcapsular cataract of the OS with surgery requirement. The patient also presented reactivation of the inflammatory activity in his OS, despite treatment.

FINDINGS:
Further examination of the patient revealed temporal and nasal conjunctival vessel tortuosity, 0.5 + of anterior chamber cells, increased superficial circular atrophy zones in 360º of the iris, some with deeper atrophy stroma fibers visualization, and a small punctate defect of transillumination. The patient also had dyscoric pupil in medium mydriasis and pseudophakia. Fundus observation for OS revealed vitreous syneresis, posterior vitreous detachment, 0.5 + vitreous cells, 0.2 optic disc excavation, and a hypopigmented retinochoroidal scar on the inferior temporal vascular arch.

LABORATORY FINDINGS:
Laboratories and aqueous humor PCR were requested, which showed the presence of VZV and Tg genetic material. The multiplex PCR qualitatively used ruled out HSV type 1, HSV type 2, Epstein-Barr virus, CMV, HHV-6 (Human Herpes Virus), HHV-7, and HHV-8 etiologies. The patient's VZV IgG level was positive.

TREATMENT:
The patient was treated with a combination of oral Pyrimethamine and Sulfadoxine, Acyclovir, and corticosteroids. The topical tropicamide was given until the resolution of inflammation in the anterior chamber was observed.

FOLLOW-UP:
After four weeks of therapy, the patient denoted subjective improvement, and resolution of the intraocular inflammation was clinically observed.

IMPRESSION:
The patient was diagnosed with ocular toxoplasmosis and IgG positive antibodies for Tg. The patient later presented with reactivation of the inflammatory activity in his OS. Further examination and laboratory results indicated the presence of VZV and Tg genetic material. The patient received treatment and showed improvement after four weeks of therapy.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
No discharge instructions are given as the patient has not been discharged.

Discharge Summary

Patient: 22-year-old male

Hospital Course:
The patient presented with bilateral muscular atrophy and tremor of the distal upper extremities with insidious onset 5 years ago. Cold exposure worsened the symptomatology. Clinical examination revealed symmetrical muscular atrophy of the hands and forearms and reduced muscular strength during flexion, extension, and abduction of the fingers and flexion, extension of the wrist, as well as reduced tendon reflexes from the affected neurotomes. Electroneurophysiology and electromyography showed signs of chronic motor neuron lesion, bilaterally. Magnetic resonance images (MRI) acquired in neutral position showed loss of cervical lordosis and focal cord atrophy at C5-C6 level. MRI acquired in flexion revealed widening of the posterior epidural space. Postcontrast images showed uniform enhancement of the epidural space. The enhancement was reduced in the neutral position. After considering all the findings, the diagnosis of HD was made, and the patient was treated conservatively.

Diagnosis:
Hirayama Disease (HD)

Summary:
The patient's symptoms were consistent with HD, and was diagnosed based on the MRI findings in neck flexion. The patient was treated conservatively and is being discharged at this time. Further monitoring and follow-up are recommended.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]
Hospital Name: [redacted]

Hospital Course:

The patient is a 23-year-old Hispanic female who presented with chest pain, dyspnoea, and fatigue. Initial investigations indicated that she may have pericarditis; however, this was ruled out after further evaluation.

Subsequent clinical findings indicated a possible cardiac cause, and an electrocardiogram (ECG) revealed ST depressions and a poor R-wave progression in the lateral precordial leads. Chest X-ray showed mild pulmonary artery congestion without pleural effusion, and a normal cardiac silhouette. Troponin T levels were elevated, and the patient underwent an urgent coronary angiography.

Coronary vasculitis with 90-95% stenosis of the proximal left anterior descending artery, 100% occlusion of the left circumflex artery, and 100% occlusion of the right posterior lateral branch with a ‘string of beads’ appearance. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMRI) showed a transmural delayed enhancement in the apex, without any viability, subendocardial delay enhancement in mid-septal, and inferolateral walls with significant viable myocardium. The CMRI results favored myocardial infarction and ischemic cardiomyopathy (ICM).

During her hospital course, the patient experienced significant hypotension and QTc prolongation, which limited the effectiveness of her medications. She was diagnosed with heart failure and underwent right heart catheterization. She could only ambulate about 10 feet on a flat surface at a slow pace without experiencing significant dyspnoea.

After a second coronary angiography showed disease progression, it was decided that the patient was unlikely to recover without a heart transplant. She underwent heart transplantation on hospital Day 16.

Pathology results of the explanted heart showed concentric intimal fibrosis with associated moderate lymphocytic inflammation of the thickened intima of all major epicardial coronary arteries. Acute multifocal microinfarction of the left ventricle was also observed.

The patient's post-transplant biopsies have not shown evidence of rejection or vasculitis, and five months after transplantation, she no longer reports any symptom-limiting activities.

Diagnosis:
- Coronary vasculitis with ischemic cardiomyopathy
- Heart failure

Treatment:
- Intravenous methylprednisolone and cyclophosphamide
- Oral hydroxychloroquine
- Heart transplantation

Follow-Up:
- The patient continues to be followed by a transplant cardiologist and rheumatologist.

Hospital Course:
The patient, an 8-year-old female child, was admitted to our department due to diminution of vision in the right eye since 5 years, with headache and vomiting since 6 months. On examination, no perception of light was observed in the right eye with normal vision in the left eye. An MRI revealed a cystic lesion at the right temporo-frontal-parietal region measuring 8 × 9 × 6.5 cm. A small rounded nodule was seen on the medial aspect of the cystic lesion at the paraventricular location. The lesion was reported as a cystic astrocytoma/ganglioglioma. Endocrine workup subsequently revealed secondary hypothyroidism.  

Treatment:
The patient underwent right fronto-temporo-parietal craniotomy for drainage and marsupialisation of the cystic lesion. The cystic fluid was yellowish and machine oil-like, containing cholesterol crystals and flecks mostly over the medial side. Histological examination revealed inflamed Rathke's cleft cyst with a cystic wall lined by a flattened to pseudostratified squamous epithelium with congested blood vessels, chronic inflammatory infiltrate in the form of lymphocyte, plasma cells, and lymphoid aggregates.

Outcomes:
Postoperatively, the patient's recovery was uneventful, and the headache and vomiting subsided completely. The patient is in regular follow-up with postoperative MRI at 6 months without any recurrence.

The patient, an 8-year-old girl, was admitted to our department because her right eye had poor vision for 5 years. She also had headaches and vomiting for 6 months. This sac measured about 8 by 9 by 6.5 centimeters. A small round bump was seen on the inner side of the sac near the fluid-filled spaces in the brain. Doctors first thought it was a type of brain tumor called a cystic astrocytoma or ganglioglioma. Blood tests later showed the patient had an underactive thyroid gland caused by another condition.

Treatment:
The patient had surgery to open the skull on the right side of the head. Doctors drained and connected the fluid-filled sac to the outside to allow it to drain. The fluid was yellow and thick like machine oil. It contained fatty crystals and small pieces mostly on the inner side. Lab tests of the tissue showed a type of fluid-filled sac called Rathke's cleft cyst. The sac wall was lined with flat to slightly raised skin-like tissue. There were also swollen blood vessels and long-term swelling with white blood cells and clumps of immune cells.

Outcomes:
After surgery, the patient recovered well without any problems. The headaches and vomiting went away completely. The patient has been coming back for regular checkups. A brain scan done 6 months after surgery showed no return of the problem.<|im_end|>

The patient showed significant improvement after the surgery. IOOA of the right eye disappeared, and esophoria of 10 PD was noted in the right eye. The patient was discharged after 1 year of follow-up.

The ophthalmology team detected mild adhesion between the IO and the surrounding tissues of the right eye, inadequate IO recession of the right eye, and IOAT of the left eye. The patient underwent surgery, during which the IO was divided into two parts. Post-surgery, the patient showed significant improvement, and IOOA of the right eye disappeared, and esophoria of 10 PD was noted in the right eye. The patient was discharged after 1 year of follow-up.

The patient's hypertropia was successfully managed with surgery. There was a significant improvement in the patient's condition, and she was discharged in a stable condition after 1 year of follow-up.

Hospital Course:
The patient was seen by the eye doctors. The exam showed that both eyes turned inward together. The right eye sat 30 units higher than normal when looking straight ahead. This increased to 45 units when the left eye looked at the same spot. The right eye also had trouble moving upward when looking to the side. The patient had surgery. During the procedure, the eye muscle was cut into two pieces. The front half was moved back 10 units. The back half was left in place. This allowed the muscle to move back to its normal path. The right eye had an incomplete muscle tightening. The left eye had a procedure to loosen the muscle.

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 17-year-old male professional motocross athlete, presented to the emergency department with isolated left thigh pain following a motocross injury. He had a history of left tibial spine avulsion fracture and chronic knee flexion contracture, and was wearing a hard-shell, hinged, knee brace measuring approximately 43 cm in length at the time of injury.

Workup:

Physical exam revealed a closed and neurovascularly intact transverse femoral shaft fracture without ecchymosis, skin changes, or open wounds. The deformity measured approximately 26 cm from the tibial tuberosity on clinical exam, and the fracture was 22 cm proximal to the center of the knee as measured on anterior-posterior (AP) radiograph. Thin-slice computed tomography (CT) was obtained to rule out femoral neck fracture, and this was negative for fracture.

Management:

The patient was placed in Buck's traction and underwent anterograde intramedullary nailing of the left femur with a femoral reconstruction nail the next morning. Routine perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis was provided, and the patient was advised for unrestricted postoperative weightbearing and one month of chemical deep vein thrombosis (DVT) prophylaxis.

Follow-up:

At one-year follow-up, the patient had returned to full activity and competitive motocross with no subsequent complications.

The patient underwent a keratolimbal allograft transplantation and cryopreserved amniotic membrane implant in his right eye due to the presence of corneal melt. Unfortunately, he persisted with severe ocular surface disease, leading to retinal and choroidal detachments in both eyes. Despite undergoing IVIG therapy, the patient's dermatologic lesions were the only ones that improved significantly. The left eye slightly improved to hand motion but the right eye displayed LP without color discrimination.

Severe ocular surface disease with retinal and choroidal detachments in both eyes.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient has been advised to follow up regularly with the ophthalmologist for monitoring and management of his ocular condition. Additionally, he has been advised to avoid any activities that may cause further damage to his eyes, including prolonged computer and smartphone use, UV exposure, and dusty environments. The patient has been informed about artificial tears and other lubricants that can provide relief for his eye symptoms.

FOLLOW-UP:
The patient will follow up with the ophthalmologist per the provider's recommendations.

Hospital Course: An 81-year-old man was admitted to our hospital due to gastric SMT that was found during a routine gastrointestinal examination. Blood examination results came within normal range, but the tumor marker levels were high: carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and α-fetoprotein (AFP). Endoscopic examination revealed a SMT located at the lower gastric body that was tested negative for malignancy. CT scan revealed a 30-mm tumor located in the gastric vestibule and an enlarged lymph node No. 6 (22 mm). After performing surgery, the patient was discharged on the postoperative day 19 without any complications.

Diagnosis: Poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with high lymphocyte proliferation in the peripheral tumor.

Treatment: Distal gastrectomy with D2 lymph node dissection and Roux-en-Y reconstruction were performed. 

Hospital Course and Disposition: The patient had refused adjuvant chemotherapy and was discharged on the postoperative day 19. No recurrence or metastasis occurred during the follow-up period of 18 months.

Prognosis: The preoperative diagnosis was gastric carcinoma with lymph node metastasis. Postoperative pathological examination demonstrated metastasis of the adenocarcinoma in 1 of 41 excised lymph nodes. The postoperative pathological stage was IIA (T2N1cM0), according to the Japanese classification of gastric cancer.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Age: 82 years
Sex: Female
Date of Admission: [redacted]
Date of Discharge: [redacted]
Diagnosis: Pneumonia, RA, Bronchiectasis, Suspected Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP)
Treatment: Ceftazidime, Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim (ST) therapy, High-dose methylprednisolone (mPSL), Salazosulfapyridine, Tacrolimus

Hospital Course:

The patient, an 82-year-old Asian Japanese female, was admitted to our hospital with shortness of breath and was diagnosed with pneumonia. She had a history of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and had been previously admitted for pneumonia and bronchiectasis. The initial treatment with intravenous ceftazidime was initiated, and the patient’s symptoms improved almost completely.

On the 14th day of admission, the patient’s respiratory status rapidly deteriorated and a chest computed tomography (CT) scan revealed areas of ground-glass opacity (GGO) bilaterally. Laboratory test values also showed elevated Krebs von den Lungen-6 (KL-6), surfactant protein-D (SP-D), and plasma (1 → 3) beta-D-glucan levels. The patient’s sputum was positive for Pneumocystis jirovecii according to polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and she was immediately treated with sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim (ST) therapy and high-dose methylprednisolone (mPSL) with empirical antibiotic therapy (ciprofloxacin).

Unfortunately, despite prompt and aggressive treatment, the patient’s respiratory status continued to deteriorate and she passed away on the 16th day of admission due to complications from suspected Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP).

Summary:

The patient, an 82-year-old Asian Japanese female with a history of RA and previous admissions for pneumonia and bronchiectasis, was admitted for pneumonia and treated with intravenous ceftazidime. However, the patient’s respiratory status rapidly deteriorated and a chest CT revealed areas of ground-glass opacity (GGO) bilaterally. Laboratory test values showed elevated levels of KL-6, SP-D, and plasma (1 → 3) beta-D-glucan, and the patient was diagnosed with suspected Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP). The patient was treated with sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim (ST) therapy and high-dose methylprednisolone (mPSL) with empirical antibiotic therapy (ciprofloxacin), but unfortunately passed away on the 16th day of admission.

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [REDACTED]
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Date of Admission: [REDACTED]
Date of Discharge: [REDACTED]
 

Hospital Course:

The patient presented to our facility with dysmenorrhea and persistent, severe pelvic pain that had been ongoing for 8 years. The patient had a history of laparotomic right adnexectomy for omolateral ovarian endometriosis and had undergone a pelvic adhesions surgical procedure at a different facility in 2009, which was unsuccessful in relieving her symptoms.

Upon admission, an ultrasound showed a right sided double cystic mass, completely separated from the uterus consistent with infraligamentary myomas. After a follow-up ultrasound, a right-sided cystic mass surrounding a smaller echoic mass was identified. This was later confirmed during a diagnostic hysteroscopy and operative laparoscopy, which revealed a unicornuate uterus with a completely separated and distant right horn with no communication with the unicornuate uterus, without any Fallopian tube or ovary, and with a small myoma. Several thick adhesions attached the right horn to the rectosigmoid and right pelvic wall.

After examination, a unilateral hemihysterectomy was performed on the patient. The right horn was removed, and the right round ligament and right vascular pedicle were coagulated and excised up to the uterine artery. Morcellation was performed, and pathologic evaluation showed the presence of myometrial tissue, hypotrophic endometrium, and focal adenomyosis.

The patient was discharged on the third day post-operation with no complications. At a 9-month follow-up, the patient reported relief from pelvic pain with no acute episodes.

 

Hospital Diagnoses:

- Dysmenorrhea 
- Severe pelvic pain 
- Infraligamentary myomas 
- Hematometra 
- Unicornuate uterus with a completely separated and distant right horn 
- Adhesions
- Focal adenomyosis

 

Hospital Procedures:

- Ultrasound 
- Hysteroscopy
- Diagnostic laparoscopy 
- Unilateral hemihysterectomy 

 

Condition of patient at discharge:

The patient was discharged with no reported complications. The patient's medical condition had significantly improved, and she reported no persistent pelvic pain. The patient was advised to schedule a follow-up with her primary care physician for further care.

The patient also had surgery at another hospital in 2009 to remove scar tissue from the pelvis. That surgery did not help with the symptoms.

When the patient arrived, an ultrasound showed a mass on the right side with two fluid-filled pockets. This mass was separate from the womb and was caused by non-cancerous growths in the muscle wall of the uterus. After another ultrasound, doctors found a mass with a smaller solid part inside. During a procedure to look inside the womb and another to open the belly, doctors found the patient had an unusual uterus shape. The right side of the uterus was fully separated from the main body and located far away. There was no tube connecting it to the other side, no ovary, and a small non-cancerous growth. Thick scar tissue had attached the right side to the lower bowel and right side of the pelvis.

After the exam, doctors removed the right side of the uterus. The right side was cut away from the tissue connecting it to the belly wall and the blood vessels. The tissue was broken down into small pieces and tested. The tests showed muscle tissue, thin womb lining, and some tissue where the womb muscle had grown into the womb lining.

The patient left the hospital three days after surgery with no problems. At a check-up 9 months later, the patient said the pelvic pain was gone and had not come back.

The patient was treated with various antimycotics and antibiotics, including itraconazole, voriconazole, amphotericin B, fluconazole, and antibacterials; however, his respiratory function continued to deteriorate.

Hospital Course:

The patient, a 46-year-old woman with a history of appendectomy and mass lesion excision from her right breast, was admitted to our hospital due to pain in the neck region and numbness in her right arm. Physical examination revealed pain at the cervical region, trigger points bilaterally on upper parts of the trapezius muscle on palpation, and restricted movements in all directions. The Spurling test on the right side was considered positive, while upper extremity muscle strength was complete, and no pathology was detected during sensory examination. Deep tendon reflexes were bilaterally normoactive, and no pathological reflex was found.

Diagnostic Assessment:

The patient was referred to our clinic for cervical magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) based on the findings of the present complaints and physical examination. Cervical MRI showed vascular structures with signal void areas that obliterated the right neural foramen at the level of C2-C3 articular disc, compressed the right C3 nerve root, and attracted our attention with curves appearance. Although signal loss consistent with degenerative changes in cervical intervertebral articular discs was observed, no pathological findings were found on both sides occupying other neural foraminal areas. Contrast-enhanced lesion was not observed after contrast injection. Contrast-enhanced MR angiography of the cervical region revealed a significant loop formation in the distal extracranial segment of the right VA. No pathological findings were found in the radius health strikes (RDUS) examination performed following MRI.

Discharge:

The patient was discharged after completion of diagnostic evaluation. The treatment plan was discussed with the patient for pain management and physical therapy. Close follow-up and monitoring of symptoms were recommended. Further evaluation was advised for any recurrence or progression of symptoms. The patient was advised to maintain good posture and avoid repetitive stress in the neck region.

The patient presented to the otolaryngology clinic with a scalp lesion with a progressive enlargement over 2 months following the MMS surgery. The biopsy confirmed the presence of invasive squamous cell carcinoma in the setting of severe solar elastosis. A surgical extirpation was performed, creating a 5 × 7 cm defect (35 cm2) that extended down to the level of the galea. Reconstructive options were discussed, and the physician elected to use vCPM. vCPM was placed on the wound in direct contact with the galea, followed by Adaptic® dressing. The patient returned to the clinic on day 7 after the first vCPM application, and a second 5 × 5 cm vCPM graft was placed on the fully granulated wound. The patient returned on day 14, at which time the defect had reduced to approximately 3 × 5 cm (15 cm2) and substantial re-epithelialization was noted. Based on the appearance of the wound, a third application was not deemed necessary. At the day 21 follow-up, the patient's wound had completely closed with minimal scarring. Six months following treatment, the wound remained closed with continued improvement in the hyperemia. The patient experienced no complications and was very pleased with the outcome.
 
DIAGNOSIS:
Invasive squamous cell carcinoma in the setting of severe solar elastosis
 
DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
The patient is advised to follow up with the physician for regular monitoring of the wound healing progress. Any signs of infection or delayed wound healing should be immediately reported to the physician.
 
FOLLOW-UP:
Follow-up appointments are scheduled with the physician for regular monitoring of the wound healing progress.

Hospital Course Summary:

Admitted for failure to thrive and extreme weight loss, a 56-year-old male presented with a history of refractory, sero-negative migratory arthritis and occasional episodes of abdominal pain and diarrhea. During initial testing, the patient exhibited neutrophilia, increased ESR, CRP levels, and abnormal WBC counts.

Over the course of several admissions, the patient's clinical condition continued to deteriorate, with thrombocytopenia and macrocytic anemia being added to his list of symptoms. Additionally, it was noted that he suffered from bilateral stenosis and total occlusion of the left internal carotid artery at the origin, among other issues.

Despite extensive testing and repeated hospital stays, the patient's condition remained mysterious, and no diagnosis was reached. However, he did test positive for Tropheryma whipplei PCR in the blood, which suggested the presence of Whipple's disease. Confirmation via small bowel biopsy was obtained, and the patient started a course of antibiotics for the disease.

As the patient's condition continued to decline, he was placed into hospice care, where he eventually passed away.

Overall, the patient's complex medical history and illness resisted easy diagnosis and treatment, despite a thorough course of testing and interventions.

Patient Name: [REDACTED]

Chief Complaint:
The patient presented to the department of Oral Medicine and Oral Surgery for the removal of fibrous hyperplasia in the mandibular vestibule caused by ill-fitting prostheses resulting in epulis fissuratum and vestibular deepening.

Hospital Course Summary:
During the extra-oral examination, a decrease in the vertical dimension of occlusion was noted. Intra-oral examination revealed hyperplastic mandibular lesion of the anterior alveolar mucosa, extending to the vestibular sulcus. Topical mouthwashes with Fungizone and chlorhexidine were prescribed to treat denture-induced hyperplasia and prosthetic candidiasis. After 3 weeks, no symptomatology was noted. Surgical resection combined with gingival graft was carried out to excise fibrous hyperplasia. The resultant lower boundary of the incision was sutured in position. Free gingival graft was carried out from the palate. The final total prosthesis was delivered to the patient 2 months later resulting in perfect integration of the prosthetic volume.

Findings:
The anatomopathological examination confirmed the clinical diagnosis of epulis fissuratum. Favorable postoperative healing was observed 2 weeks later during the removal of sutures.

Treatment:
Topical mouthwashes with Fungizone and chlorhexidine were prescribed to treat denture-induced hyperplasia and prosthetic candidiasis. Antibiotics and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs were administered for 5 days. Chlorhexidine mouth rinse was prescribed twice a day for 2 weeks. The patient was asked to have a soft diet, limited facial movement for 14 days, no brushing around the surgical site for 3 days after the procedure, and to place an ice pack on the lower lip.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient should continue to follow the prescribed soft diet, use chlorhexidine mouth rinse twice a day, and avoid brushing around the surgical site for 3 days after the procedure. The patient should also place an ice pack on the lower lip as needed. In case of discomfort, the patient is advised to contact the hospital. 

Follow-Up:
The patient should have regular follow-up sessions with the oral medicine and oral surgery department to ensure there is no recurrence of the fibrous hyperplasia and that the total prosthesis remains stable and well-extended. 

Signed, 
[REDACTED]
Oral Surgeon

Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with gradually progressive gait difficulty and frequent falls, combined with mental “slowness”, and reduced attention. In addition, urinary incontinence was noted. The patient was unresponsive to L-dopa treatment. Clinical examination showed limb bradykinesia and rigidity, myoclonic jerks were observed in the upper limbs, cortical sensory loss, sensory neglect, and primitive reflexes (especially grasping). Neuropsychological testing revealed moderate-stage dementia, including significant frontal, amnestic, and visuoconstructive components, while significant upper limb apraxia was present. The patient was diagnosed with corticobasal syndrome based on these clinical findings.

Diagnostic Findings:
Neuroimaging revealed an increased Evans index, acute callosal angle, and periventricular caps, suggestive of normal pressure hydrocephalus. Some degree of cerebral small vessel disease was also evident. The spinal taping test resulted in a significant improvement of cognition, but there was no change in gait. Analysis of CSF biomarkers showed reduced Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio, together with increased τP-181 and τT, compatible with the presence of AD.

Overall Assessment and Plan:
The patient met clinical criteria for corticobasal syndrome and had additional findings suggestive of normal pressure hydrocephalus and cerebral small vessel disease. The patient was treated symptomatically for her condition while in the hospital. The patient was discharged with recommendations for outpatient follow-up with their primary care physician and a neurologist. Further diagnostic workup was recommended, including imaging studies and a follow-up evaluation of the CSF biomarkers.

Discharge Summary:

Admission Date: [date]
Discharge Date: [date]

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the hospital because of slowly worsening trouble walking and many falls. The patient also had slow thinking, trouble paying attention, and could not control their bladder. The patient did not respond well to a medicine called L-dopa. The doctor's exam showed slow movement and stiffness in the arms and legs. The patient had sudden muscle jerks in the arms. The patient had trouble sensing touch and ignoring it on one side of the body. The patient also had basic reflexes that were not normal. Brain and memory tests showed moderate memory loss and thinking problems. The patient had trouble with planning movements and drawing shapes. The patient also had trouble doing tasks with the hands. Based on these findings, the patient was diagnosed with corticobasal syndrome. This is a brain disorder that affects movement and thinking.

Diagnostic Findings:
Brain scans showed signs of fluid buildup in the brain. This suggested normal pressure hydrocephalus, which is a condition where fluid gets trapped in the brain. There were also signs of small blood vessel disease in the brain. A spinal tap test helped improve thinking and memory. However, it did not help with walking. A test of the fluid around the brain and spine showed low levels of a protein called Aβ42. It also showed high levels of two proteins called τP-181 and τT. These results point to a type of brain disease called Alzheimer's disease.

Overall Assessment and Plan:
The patient met the signs for corticobasal syndrome. The patient also had signs of fluid buildup in the brain and small blood vessel disease. The patient received treatment to manage symptoms while in the hospital. The patient was sent home with instructions to see their regular doctor and a brain and nerve specialist. More tests are needed, including brain scans and another spinal fluid test.<|im_end|>

Discharge Summary:

Patient Name: [redacted]
Medical Record Number: [redacted]
Date of Admission: February 12, 2019
Date of Discharge: March 5, 2019

Admission Diagnosis:
Henoch-Schonlein Purpura (HSP) 

Medical History:
History of haemorrhoidectomy

Hospital Course Summary:
On admission, the patient presented with abdominal pain, hematochezia and rash. Laboratory tests revealed an elevated WBC count, high GR%, high PLT count and elevated GGT levels. Abdominal ultrasound showed cholestasis and cholecystitis, while CT scans showed localized wall thickening in the small intestine along with pericardial effusion, pelvic effusion, and abdominal lymph node enlargement. The patient was diagnosed with HSP according to the 2006 European League Against Rheumatism and the Paediatric Rheumatology European Society criteria. 

On February 13, 2019, the patient had increased heart rate and dark red bloody stool twice again with the total amount of about 400 mL. Laboratory tests showed an elevated WBC count and high GR%. Considering the gastrointestinal bleeding, glucocorticoid therapy was initiated. The patient was initially treated with somatostatin and pantoprazole sodium, followed by octreotide, levofloxacin, calcium gluconate and human albumin. Glucocorticoid therapy with methylprednisolone was also administered twice a day. 

By February 15, 2019, the patient's abdominal pain was effectively relieved and heart rate normalized with an average of 80 beats per minute. However, the patient continued to experience symptoms of hematochezia intermittently. By February 19, 2019, the patient's abdominal pain and hematochezia resolved. Several drugs were prescribed, and methylprednisolone dosage was gradually reduced.

However, the patient was readmitted on March 13, 2019, due to pain recurrence. Laboratory tests showed elevated WBC count and GR%. Methylprednisolone and esomeprazole were given intravenously to relieve symptoms, and the patient showed improvements by April 1, 2019. 

On April 2, 2019, the patient was discharged and advised on a stepwise reduction of dosage of oral methylprednisolone. A follow-up appointment was scheduled to assess the patient's condition. 

Discharge Instructions:
• The patient was advised to continue taking oral methylprednisolone, as prescribed, and to follow the stepwise reduction in dosage.
• The patient was instructed to monitor their condition and report any symptoms such as abdominal pain, hematochezia, rash, vomiting, or fever.
• The patient was advised to maintain a balanced diet, drink sufficient fluids, and avoid foods that may cause gastrointestinal bleeding. 
• The patient was instructed to avoid activities that may lead to injury or trauma, as certain types of physical trauma may trigger a HSP recurrence.
• The patient was advised to promptly notify healthcare providers prior to starting any new medications or supplements, or if any unusual symptoms develop.
• Follow-up appointments were scheduled to monitor the patient's progress and adjust the medication regimen, if necessary. 

Conditions at Discharge:
Henoch-Schonlein Purpura (HSP) 

Medical Recommendations:
Continue taking oral methylprednisolone, as prescribed, and reduce dosage gradually. Monitor for symptoms and avoid activities that may trigger recurrences of HSP. Follow up with healthcare provider for monitoring and medication adjustment.

Hospital Course:
The patient, an 89-year-old male, was admitted with complaints of cough and haemoptysis. The patient had a history of bronchiectasis in the right middle lobe bronchus for 30 years. One month before admission, the patient suffered a massive haemoptysis, which was controlled through bronchial artery embolization. However, haemoptysis recurred, and the patient was admitted to our hospital. Bronchoscopy performed on the patient showed active bleeding from the right middle lobe bronchus, which was temporarily controlled through haemostatic medications and cryotherapy.

Management:
On the next day, an 8 mm x 3 cm silicon stent was deployed using biopsy forceps after multiple attempts. Another customized stent with a diameter of about 6 mm was placed from the right intermediate bronchus to the right lower lobe bronchus, to avoid stent migration. Bleeding stopped promptly after the placement of stents. The patient received successive antibiotic therapy, and no obvious complication of obstructive pneumonia developed.

Outcome:
One month after the operation of stents implantation, the patient experienced complete atelectasis of the right middle and lower lobe with the complication of right pleural effusion. The patient received further surgery to remove the silicon stent inside the right intermediate bronchus. Thoracic puncture and drainage were done after stent extraction for relieving pleural effusion. Post-operation CT scan reviewed the patient's chest and revealed the right lower lobe recruitment. The patient did not experience any more haemoptysis during the three-month follow-up time and left the hospital ten days after stent extraction.
results.simplified:
Hospital Course:
The patient is an 89-year-old man. He came to the hospital because he was coughing up blood. The patient had a condition called bronchiectasis for 30 years. This means his airways in the right middle part of his lung were wider than normal. One month before coming to the hospital, the patient coughed up a large amount of blood. Doctors stopped the bleeding by blocking a blood vessel near the lung. But the bleeding started again, so the patient came to our hospital. Doctors used a small camera to look inside the airways. They found bleeding from the right middle part of the lung. They stopped the bleeding for now with medicine and freezing treatment.

Management:
The next day, doctors placed two small tubes called stents to keep the airways open. The first stent was about 8 mm wide and 3 cm long. It was made of a soft material. Doctors had to try several times to get it in the right place. The second stent was smaller, about 6 mm wide. It was placed from the middle airway to the lower part of the right lung. This helped stop the stent from moving out of place. The bleeding stopped right after the stents were put in. The patient received medicines to fight infection. No lung infection developed.

Outcome:
One month after the stents were put in, the right middle and lower parts of the lung collapsed. The patient also had fluid building up around the right lung. The patient had surgery to remove the first stent from the middle airway. Doctors used a needle to drain the fluid from around the lung. A scan of the chest showed the lower part of the right lung was working again. The patient did not cough up any more blood during three months of checkups. The patient left the hospital ten days after the second stent was removed.<|im_end|>

The patient is a 20-year-old female diagnosed with pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and von Willebrand Disease (vWD) who presented to the clinic at 13 weeks of gestation. The patient underwent a caesarian section at 26 weeks of gestation due to decompensated PAH and fetal bradycardia. Post-procedure, the patient went into cardiac arrest and was placed on femoral-femoral venoarterial (VA) ECMO for life support. She remained in the ICU for an extended period of time and required continuous inotropes and vasopressors support. After a prolonged stay in the hospital, she was discharged on postoperative day 101.

Discharge Diagnosis:

1. Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH)
2. Von Willebrand Disease (vWD)

Procedures:

1. emergent caesarian section
2. femoral-femoral venoarterial (VA) ECMO
3. pericardial window

Hospital Course Summary:

The patient was diagnosed with PAH and vWD, and presented at 13 weeks of gestation. Despite counseling sessions, the patient decided to continue her pregnancy, and all teratogenic medications were stopped while tadalafil and treprostinil were continued. The patient was admitted and transitioned from SC treprostinil to intravenous epoprostenol. The patient did well on this regimen until 25 weeks of gestation when she presented to the emergency department with complaints of recurrent hemoptysis and acute respiratory distress. Due to concerns of further decompensation, an emergent caesarian section was performed, and a healthy infant was delivered. However, during the closure of the hysterotomy, the patient went into cardiac arrest and was placed on VA ECMO for life support. The patient remained in the ICU for a prolonged period and required continuous inotropes and vasopressors support. After successful management, the patient was weaned off ECMO support and had a prolonged stay in the hospital. The patient was subsequently discharged on postoperative day 101.

results.original:
HOSPITAL ADMISSION SUMMARY:
Fifty-year-old female patient referred to cardiology department for hypertension diagnosis at the age of 42 presented with exertional dyspnoea. Physical examination revealed lower leg pitting oedema, while 12-lead ECG showed repolarisation abnormalities in the inferior leads and precordial leads. Transthoracic echocardiography showed concentric hypertrophy with a maximal thickness of 11 mm at the left ventricle. Laboratory examination showed proteinuria, with genetic analysis showing an abnormal variant in the GLA gene. Coronary angiography revealed no organic stenosis. Endomyocardial biopsy of the right ventricular wall demonstrated vacuolated myocytes in cardiomyocytes, while late gadolinium enhancement was seen at the basal of the left inferolateral ventricular wall.
DISCHARGE DIAGNOSIS:
The patient was diagnosed as having Anderson-Fabry disease (AFD) with cardiac hypertrophy.
HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:
Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) with aglasidase-β was initiated following genetic testing, as the patient was diagnosed with AFD. Further medication therapy including enalapril and diltiazem was administered, while beta-blocker was not used. After 12 months of ERT and medication therapy, proteinuria disappeared, while lyso-Gb3 level did not decrease and TTE findings of cardiac hypertrophy remained unchanged. The patient was discharged in stable condition and advised to follow up with cardiology department.

An ultrasound of the heart showed the heart muscle had thickened to 11 mm in one area. Blood tests showed protein in the urine. Gene testing found a change in a gene called GLA. A test to look at the heart arteries showed no blockages. A small tissue sample was taken from the right side of the heart. It showed damaged heart muscle cells with small bubbles inside. A special scan also showed scarring at the bottom of the left side of the heart.

The patient was diagnosed with Anderson-Fabry disease. This is a rare condition where a fatty substance builds up in the body. It caused the heart muscle to become thick.

HOSPITAL COURSE SUMMARY:
The patient started enzyme replacement therapy with a medicine called aglasidase-β. This treatment was started after the gene test confirmed the disease. The patient also took two medicines called enalapril and diltiazem. A medicine to slow the heart rate was not used. After 12 months of treatment, the protein in the urine went away. However, a fatty substance in the blood did not go down. The heart ultrasound still showed the heart muscle was thick. The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient was told to come back for follow-up visits with the heart doctor.<|im_end|>

The patient was admitted to our hospital due to jaundice of skin and sclera along with epigastric pain for two weeks.

Upon admission, the patient underwent a series of examinations that included enhanced abdominal and pelvic CT scans, chest X-ray, abdominal ultrasound, tumor markers, liver and renal function, and coagulation function test. Post the tests, the patient was incorrectly diagnosed with pancreatic neuroendocrine neoplasm, and a pancreaticoduodenectomy was performed. The diagnosis was later changed to pancreatic ACC with invasion of the duodenum and distal common bile upon biopsy.

Treatment:
Gemcitabine-based regime was administered to the patient one month after the operation.

Outcome:
The patient was closely monitored every three months by physical examination, laboratory tests and imaging examinations. Nine months after the operation, the patient had no relapsing symptoms.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Pancreatic ACC

Discharge Instructions:
The patient has been discharged as he did not show any relapsing symptoms at the time of discharge. Follow-ups will be needed as advised by your physician.

Follow-up plan:
It is recommended that the patient continues his follow-ups with his physician, laboratory tests and imaging examinations as needed.

Hospital Course:

The patient presented with a 24-hour history of lower abdominal pain and was diagnosed with a small bowel obstruction in the midabdomen encased by a thickened peritoneum. Conservative treatment was attempted but was unsuccessful, and an exploratory laparotomy was performed. During the procedure, the entire small intestine was found to be encapsulated by a thick membrane forming a cocoon-like lump. The fibrous membrane was excised and the small bowel loops were released. The patient had no postoperative complications and was discharged on postoperative day seven.

Diagnosis:

Small bowel obstruction in the midabdomen encased by a thickened peritoneum due to peritoneal fibrosis.

Follow-Up:

The patient will be monitored for signs of recurrence, but as of the most recent follow-up 28 months after surgery, the patient has shown no signs of recurrence.

Overall Impression:

The patient presented with a rare case of small bowel obstruction due to peritoneal fibrosis which required surgical intervention. The surgery was successful, and the patient had an uneventful postoperative course with no signs of recurrence at the most recent follow-up.

Patient: 37-year-old African-American female with Hgb S/OArab

Hospital Course:
The patient presented to the clinic with sudden decrease in vision of her right eye. On examination, her vision was hand motion in the right eye and 20/20 in the left eye. Pupils were reactive to light, with no relative afferent pupillary defect. A dense vitreous hemorrhage was observed in the right eye causing a poor view to the posterior pole and periphery. Additionally, the patient had a cup/disc ratio of 0.5 and sclerotic retinal vessels in the temporal and nasal periphery. 

Treatment and Management:
The patient underwent 23-G pars plana vitrectomy with membrane segmentation and endolaser in the right eye for the vitreous hemorrhage. Delamination was avoided where membranes were firmly adherent to the vascular arcades.

Follow-up:
At the 1-month follow-up appointment, the patient reported sudden painless vision loss 2 days prior in her left eye. Her vision at this time was 20/100 in right eye and 20/50 in left eye. No relative afferent pupillary defect was noted. Fundus examination of the left eye revealed patchy areas of retinal whitening around the fovea, consistent with multiple branch retinal artery occlusions (BRAO). OCT done a few weeks later showed residual tractional membranes in the right eye. In the left eye, inner retinal atrophy consistent with previous BRAO was seen. 

Recommendations:
The patient was offered a fluorescein angiogram at this time to confirm the diagnosis, but she declined. Further monitoring is recommended to ensure proper management of her condition.
results.simplified:
Patient: 37-year-old African-American female with sickle cell disease and a blood disorder called S/OArab

Hospital Course:
The patient came to the clinic because her right eye vision suddenly got worse. Her right eye could only see hand movements up close. Her left eye had normal vision. The pupils in both eyes reacted to light normally. There was heavy bleeding inside the right eye. This blocked the doctor's view of the back and outer parts of the eye. The patient also had signs of high pressure in the eye and hardened blood vessels in the outer parts of both eyes.

Treatment and Management:
The patient had surgery on the right eye to remove the gel inside the eye and treat the bleeding. The doctor used a laser to seal the blood vessels. The doctor carefully separated the tissue where it was stuck to the blood vessels.

Follow-up:
At the 1-month checkup, the patient said her left eye vision suddenly got worse 2 days before. She had no pain. Her right eye vision was reduced to 20/100. Her left eye vision was reduced to 20/50. The pupils in both eyes reacted normally to light. The doctor looked at the left eye and found white patches of damaged tissue around the center of the eye. This meant several blood vessels in the eye had blocked. A scan done a few weeks later showed some scar tissue was still pulling on the eye in the right eye. The left eye showed damage to the inner layer of the eye from the earlier blood vessel blockage.

Recommendations:
The doctor suggested a special dye test to confirm the diagnosis. The patient said no to this test. The care team should keep watching the patient closely to make sure she gets the right care.<|im_end|>

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Admission date: [DATE OF ADMISSION]
Discharge date: [DATE OF DISCHARGE]

Patient: [FULL NAME], [AGE], White, Male

Chief Complaint:
Abdominal pain

Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted with abdominal pain of sharp and colicky nature. The pain started in the mid-lower abdomen and then became generalized, non-radiating, and associated with nausea but no vomiting. He had noticed blood in his stool occasionally in the past, but his last bowel movement, which was three days prior to his presentation, did not have any blood. His past medical history was significant for hypertension and peripheral vascular disease, status post aorto-femoral bypass graft six years prior.

On physical examination, the abdomen was non-distended, but diffusely tender and rigid, more on the left lower quadrant. Bowel sounds were hypoactive. Rectal examination revealed an empty vault. Laboratory results showed a WBC count of 9.0 x 103 / mm3 with 94.5% neutrophils, hemoglobin of 9.0 gm/dl, and hematocrit of 31.3%. The liver and pancreatic enzymes were not elevated. ABG showed no acidosis, and lactic acid level was not elevated. X-ray showed a non-obstructive bowel pattern without any free air. Abdominal CT with oral and IV gastrograffin showed findings consistent with complete mid to distal small bowel obstruction secondary to a closed loop obstruction. Emergent laparotomy was performed, and the finding was a Meckel's diverticulum that had formed a band around a portion of the small bowel causing it to twist upon itself with subsequent necrosis. Histopathology showed Meckel's diverticulum with hemorrhagic necrosis and benign intestinal tissue with necrosis.

The patient had uneventful postoperative recovery and was discharged on [DATE OF DISCHARGE] to follow-up with his primary care physician.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Complete mid to distal small bowel obstruction secondary to a closed loop obstruction caused by a Meckel's diverticulum.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient was advised to follow-up with his primary care physician for further management and care. No specific dietary or activity restrictions were recommended. The patient was also advised to return to the hospital if he experiences any new or worsening symptoms.

Follow-up:
The patient was advised to make an appointment with his primary care physician to receive further management and care.

The patient, a 38-year-old male, presented to our emergency department with a low-grade fever, headache, dizziness, blurry vision, and red eyes over the preceding week. Ophthalmologic examination revealed mild diffuse conjunctival injection and bilateral sluggish pupil reaction. Funduscopic examination displayed optic disc swelling and hyperemia with choroidal whitish lesions. Further evaluation using B-mode ultrasound and optical coherence tomography demonstrated significant bilateral posterior choroidal thickening and exudative retinal detachment. Brain MRI displayed bilateral posterior predominant nodular choroid thickening with post-contrast enhancement, associated episcleral thickening, and enhancement inseparable from the optic nerve sheath at its globe insertion. Bilateral inner ear membranous labyrinth enhancement was noted as well. Finally, diffuse supra- and infratentorial leptomeningeal enhancement predominantly along the cerebellar folia and basal cisterns were observed. Laboratory evaluation for infectious, granulomatous, and other autoimmune diseases was unrevealing. The patient clinical presentation and the associated constellation of ophthalmologic and radiologic findings supported the diagnosis of VKHD. High-dose oral corticosteroid treatment was initiated, and the patient's symptoms improved. The patient was symptom-free at his one-year follow-up evaluation.

Hospital Course Summary:
The patient, a 38-year-old male, was admitted to our hospital with symptoms of fever, headache, dizziness, blurry vision, and red eyes. He was diagnosed with VKHD based on his presentation and radiologic findings. High-dose oral corticosteroid treatment was initiated and the patient's symptoms improved during his hospital stay. On his one-year follow-up evaluation, the patient was found to be symptom-free. Throughout his hospital course, laboratory evaluation for infectious, granulomatous, and other autoimmune diseases was unrevealing.

Medical History:
The patient is a 39-year-old Vietnamese male with no significant past medical history.

Chief Complaint:
Sharp 5/10 right upper quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and chills

Admission:
The patient was admitted with an increased anion gap, an acute kidney injury, alkaline phosphatase, AST, ALT, ferritin, and a white blood cell count.

Diagnostic Results:
CT scan with contrast of the chest and abdomen showed a large, complex mass in the right lobe of the liver with multiple septations. Ultrasound of the abdomen showed a heterogeneous, hypoechoic, multiseptated hepatic mass with internal vascularity.

Treatment:
The patient was started on vancomycin and piperacillin/tazobactam with fluid resuscitation consisting of crystalloids.

Medical Intensive Care Unit:
The patient was transferred to the medical intensive care unit (MICU) due to tachypnea, tachycardia, and hypoxia.

Arterial Blood Gas:
ABG showed a pH of 7.34 and a bicarbonate of 14. The patient was placed on bilevel positive airway pressure ventilation and continued on antibiotics.

Repeat CT:
The repeat CT of the thorax and abdomen showed areas of consolidated infiltrate with some associated atelectatic change in the posterior right lower lobe.

Magnetic Resonance Imaging:
MRI showed a peripherally enhancing mass in the anterior segment of the right hepatic lobe that measured 3.5 cm x 3.3 cm with areas of central septations.

Blood Cultures:
Blood cultures grew Klebsiella pneumonia.

Discharge:
The patient was discharged after 11 days. A repeat CT of the abdomen showed interval improvement of the abscess. The patient was referred to an infectious disease specialist for follow-up.

The patient was moved to the intensive care unit because of fast breathing, fast heartbeat, and low oxygen levels.

A second CT scan of the chest and belly showed signs of infection in the lungs with some collapsed lung tissue.

An MRI scan showed a mass in the front part of the right side of the liver. The mass measured about 3.5 cm by 3.3 cm and had walls inside it.

Blood Cultures:
Blood tests found a type of bacteria called Klebsiella pneumonia.

Discharge:
The patient was sent home after 11 days. A follow-up CT scan of the belly showed the liver infection had gotten better. The patient was sent to a doctor who specializes in infections for follow-up care.<|im_end|>

Discharge Summary:

Admission date: [Date]
Discharge date: [Date]

Patient name: [Patient Name]
Date of birth: [Date of Birth]
Medical record number: [Medical Record Number]

Hospital course: 
The patient was admitted to our Neurology department with complaints of progressively worsening headache, vertigo, tinnitus and bilateral hearing loss, which had been present for 3 years. The patient had been referred to neurology clinic after cranial MRI showed T2 hyper-intensities suggestive of demyelinating lesions. The neurologic examination was normal except for bilateral hearing loss. Further diagnostic imaging showed multiple cystic lesions on the right and left frontal convexity accompanied by pathological meningeal enhancement and bilateral symmetric vasogenic edema in the frontoparietal and temporal regions. A comprehensive blood survey showed no significant abnormalities except for a slightly positive nucleolar pattern of fluorescent antinuclear antibody and a positive Rose Bengal test for Brucella. Further diagnostic tests were performed which included Tuberculin skin test, blood, and urine cultures, which were all negative. A lumbar puncture was performed and showed normal cell count with normal protein and glucose levels.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with CNS tuberculosis after a cranial biopsy demonstrated necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with a holding caseous center. 

Treatment:
The patient was started on Rifampicin, doxycycline, and ceftriaxone empirically with the early diagnosis of neurobrucellosis. Later, Doxycycline was stopped, and isoniazide, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide were added to her treatment, with a final diagnosis of CNS tuberculosis. She responded well to the treatment, and it was continued for 12 months.

Follow-up care:
The patient was discharged from the clinic and recommended a cochlear implantation for bilateral sensorineural hearing loss after eradication of the infection. Follow-up appointments were scheduled every 6 months for clinical and radiological evaluation. No improvement in the hearing loss was observed.

Disposition:
The patient was discharged in stable condition with improved headache and MRI findings. The patient was advised to continue the antituberculosis treatment and keep up with the routine follow-up appointments.

Hospital course:
The patient came to the brain and nerve department with worsening headaches, dizziness, ringing in the ears, and hearing loss in both ears. These problems had been getting worse for 3 years. The patient was sent to this department after a brain scan showed signs of damage to the protective covering of the nerves. The brain and nerve exam was normal except for hearing loss in both ears. More scans showed several fluid-filled pockets in the front and side parts of the brain. The scans also showed swelling around the brain and a thickening of the brain covering. Blood tests were mostly normal. However, tests for a type of infection called Brucella came back slightly positive. More tests were done to check for tuberculosis. These included a skin test, blood tests, and urine tests. All of these tests came back negative. A sample of spinal fluid was taken and tested. The results were normal for cell count, protein, and sugar levels.

Diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with tuberculosis of the brain and spinal cord. This was confirmed by taking a small tissue sample from the brain. The sample showed swelling and damage with a center of dying tissue.

Treatment:
The patient was first given three medicines to fight the infection: Rifampicin, doxycycline, and ceftriaxone. These were started because doctors thought the patient had brain tuberculosis and a possible ear infection. Later, doxycycline was stopped. Three other medicines were added: isoniazide, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide. The final diagnosis was tuberculosis of the brain and spinal cord. The patient responded well to treatment. The patient took these medicines for 12 months.

Follow-up care:
The patient left the clinic and was told to get a hearing device placed in both ears. This is because the hearing loss was caused by nerve damage. The infection needed to be fully cleared before this could happen. The patient was told to come back for checkups every 6 months. These visits would include exams and scans. The hearing loss did not get better.

Disposition:
The patient left the hospital in stable condition with less severe headaches and improved brain scan results. The patient was told to keep taking the tuberculosis medicines. The patient was also told to keep coming to all follow-up appointments.<|im_end

This 26-year-old male patient was admitted to the dermatology clinic of Tanta University hospital presenting with severe burning sensation during urination, dysuria, penile discharge, and testicular tenderness, which were determined to be symptomatic of gonorrhea. The patient was treated for 7 days and a dual therapy was initiated with gentamicin and azithromycin to attain complete recovery of the patient. Additional treatment which involved the use of normal saline solution to irrigate the patients' eyes four times per day was also initiated.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY:

Patient Name: Anonymous

Medical Record Number: XXXXXX

Date of Admission: XXXXXXX           
Date of Discharge: XXXXXXX

Admitting Diagnosis: Incomplete spinal cord injury 

Hospital Course:

The patient was admitted to the Spinal Cord Injury Center at Balgrist University Hospital, Switzerland, with an incomplete spinal cord injury. Clinical examination revealed the patient was suffering from central cord syndrome, at-level neuropathic pain, and mechanical allodynia (brush allodynia) on both forearms. Light touch was normal within the cervical segments, and sharp dull discrimination was preserved while the intensity of the pinprick stimulus was only mildly attenuated. The upper extremity motor score (left: 11/25, right: 11/25) showed a profound weakness of elbow extensors, and no voluntary muscle activity in the finger flexors and abductors bilaterally. The lower extremity motor score was normal (left: 25/25, right: 25/25). Biceps and brachioradialis reflexes, as well as knee-jerk and ankle-jerk reflexes were bilaterally exaggerated. The Babinski sign was negative. Muscle tone was normal without signs of spasticity. Coordination and gait showed discrete signs of ataxia.

Diagnostic testing revealed a multi-segmental (C5-C7), longitudinal “snake eye” appearance myelopathy. Ulnar somatosensory evoked potentials (SEPs) and sensory nerve conduction studies were normal. Noxious mechanical stimuli were applied with a 256 mN pinprick stimulator, and CHEPs were recorded, which appeared to be normal at C4 and T4 with high pain ratings to the heat stimulation, but almost complete loss of painful heat sensation and absent CHEPs at the level of C6.

The patient was treated for the duration of their stay in the hospital. Written informed consent was obtained from the patient before conducting any study, and the study was approved by the “Kantonale Ethikkommission Zürich”.

Discharge Status:

The patient was discharged from the hospital in stable condition. The patient’s condition and prognosis were discussed with them and their families, and the necessary education and instructions regarding self-care, follow-up care, and rehabilitation were provided. The patient and their families were advised to seek immediate medical attention if there were any signs of deterioration in their condition.

Follow-Up:

The patient was instructed to follow up with their primary care physician or specialist for ongoing management of their condition. Any changes in the patient's condition should be reported immediately to their healthcare provider.

Signed,

Dr. Anonymous

The patient also had pain when light touch was applied to both forearms. Light touch was normal in the neck area. The patient could still feel sharp and dull touches normally. However, the pain from a strong pinprick was only slightly reduced. Muscle strength in the arms was very weak. The patient could not move the elbow backward or bend the fingers on both hands. The legs had normal muscle strength. Reflexes in the arms and legs were stronger than normal on both sides. A test for nerve damage in the feet was normal. Muscle stiffness was normal with no signs of muscle tightness. Balance and walking showed mild problems with coordination.

Tests showed damage to several parts of the spinal cord in the neck area. A special scan showed the spinal cord looked like a snake eye pattern. Nerve tests for feeling and movement were normal. A strong heat test was used to check pain response. The test results were normal at the neck and upper back levels. The patient rated the heat as very painful. The patient could not feel painful heat at the neck level and had no nerve response there.

The patient received treatment during their hospital stay. The patient gave written permission before any testing was done. The study was approved by the “Kantonale Ethikkommission Zürich”.

The patient left the hospital in stable condition. The patient's condition and expected recovery were discussed with them and their families. Discharge Summary:

Admitting Diagnosis: Altered Mental Status
Final Diagnosis: Pancreatic Cancer with Hypercalcemia, Encephalopathy and Cerebral Spinal Fluid (CSF) Pleocytosis

Hospital Course:

A 67-year-old, right-handed female patient was admitted to the emergency department presenting with altered mental status. Neurologic examination was very limited secondary to sedation. Per family, the patient had a 2-week history of malaise, generalized weakness, headache, incontinence, and eventually altered mental status. On examination, her vital signs included temperature: 98.3°F, pulse: 99/min, BP: 201/94 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation: 98% after intubation. Initial laboratory work demonstrated a normal complete blood count (CBC) and normal coagulation. Blood chemistry was notable for an elevated calcium of 14.5 mg/dL. Liver function tests demonstrated an elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP) of 208 U/L; renal function laboratories were within normal limits. Cerebral spinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed a white blood cell count (WBC) of 91 with a lymphocyte value of 90, and a protein of 32. CSF testing for herpes simplex virus, coccidioidomycosis, cryptococcus, West Nile virus, and syphilis were all negative. Head computer tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance venography (MRV) were negative. Electroencephalography (EEG) repeatedly showed generalized slowing. On day 2, MRI fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) showed abnormal posterior subcortical signal density with bilateral thalamic pulvinar involvement.

During the hospital course, the patient was admitted to the ICU and underwent aggressive blood pressure management. Her blood pressure was brought down to 140/57 mm Hg over 4 days. Her mentation slowly improved over 1 week; she was extubated on day 8. Repeat blood work showed a normal chemistry with a calcium of 7.4 mg/dL. Her ALP had decreased to 125 U/L. Her brain was followed with serial MRIs. By day 10, her FLAIR abnormality had almost resolved. Repeat EEG showed delta activity. A repeat CSF analysis showed a WBC of 2 and a protein of 24. NMDA receptor antibody was negative both in serum and CSF. Prion study was negative with negative RT-QuIC and no 14-3-3 protein.

The patient was diagnosed with pancreatic cancer, requiring further management. The patient neurologically continued to improve to a close to baseline state. She was discharged home after a 20-day stay in the hospital.

Follow-up and Recommendations:

A 1-month postdischarge phone call revealed the patient had remained at her baseline mentation. Unfortunately, the patient eventually passed away from her pancreatic cancer 5 months later.

Recommendations include close monitoring of the patient's pancreatic cancer and management of hypercalcemia if it recurs. Additionally, neurological follow-up may be needed to monitor any residual deficits or progression of encephalopathy.

The patient was admitted with back pain and claudication pain in both lower limbs. After evaluation, she was found to have degenerative scoliosis with malalignment of global sagittal and coronal balance with lumbar canal stenosis. Surgical intervention was warranted after the failure of an appropriate conservative trial. She underwent instrumentation from T10 to pelvis and interbody fusion was done at multiple levels using the standard posterior approach. Post-operatively, the patient was doing fine and was kept on regular follow-up.

During the six-year follow-up period, the patient presented with generalized back pain. Evaluation revealed breakage of both rods at the dorso-lumbar junction and breakage of the left side rod between the S1 screw and the S2-iliac screw. "Vacuum phenomenon" can be seen in the disc space corresponding with the site of breakage of the rod on CT scan. The solid bony union is appreciated in the rest of the construct. Conservative management was chosen, involving trigger point injections, analgesics, and physiotherapy. 

Outcome:
After five years of conservative management, the patient was completely asymptomatic. No further treatment was necessary, and the patient was discharged on [redacted] after a [redacted] day hospital stay with recommendations for regular follow-up care.

The problem was resolved by ensuring a lower interference location for the programming.

A 67-year-old man with essential tremor underwent implantation of a DBS system. During his follow-up visit, there were difficulties in communicating between the IPG and the programming device because of radiofrequency interference. The issue was evaluated by taking RF readings at every corner of the room, where a higher reading was found at the patient's location and resolved by moving the patient to a new location for programming.

A further investigation of MRCP revealed the absence of the dorsal pancreatic duct and a short duct of Wirsung running into the major papilla. On the basis of these findings, a diagnosis of complete ADP was evident.
Low-fat diet was recommended, and pancreatic enzyme supplements as well as mosapride citrate were given with meals to facilitate the digestive process. The pain gradually resolved and went away in 7 days after the treatment. 
One month later, a standard mixed-meal tolerance test was performed to evaluate β-cell function. According to the patient’s medical history and laboratory results, the diagnosis of “Specific types of diabetes due to other causes” was established. The patient received insulin therapy (insulin glargine 12 units at bedtime and biosynthetic human insulin 16 units with meals) and was followed up.

The patient was discharged in stable condition on MM/DD/YYYY.

The patient was advised to continue following a low-fat diet, and pancreatic enzyme supplements as well as mosapride citrate were suggested to be taken with meals to facilitate the digestive process.
