Hospital Course:
A 24-year-old male was admitted to the emergency department due to multiple gunshot wounds. Physical examination showed that the patient was hemodynamically stable, and no additional findings were noted. 
Further workup including CT scans led to the discovery of a bullet in the right ventricle. Cardiothoracic surgery and cardiology were consulted, and an attempt at endovascular retrieval was made. However, due to the bullet being embedded in the right ventricular trabeculations, a median sternotomy was performed with cardiopulmonary bypass and cardioplegic arrest to successfully remove the bullet. The patient was subsequently transferred to the ICU for further observation. 
On the sixth postoperative day, the patient developed a fever, and further imaging revealed a large pericardial effusion and moderate right pleural effusion with right lower lobe collapse. Pericardiocentesis was performed, and a drain was left in place to evacuate the effusion. The patient's condition continued to improve, and he was stable for transfer to a comprehensive rehabilitation center on the fifteenth postoperative day.

At first, doctors tried to remove the bullet through a blood vessel. This did not work because the bullet was stuck in the heart muscle. So doctors opened the chest through the breastbone. Clinical Findings:
On admission, the patient presented with mild headache and left inguinal lymph node swelling, with no erythema, rash, or wheal. Laboratory tests revealed low platelet and white blood cell counts, atypical lymphocytes, and elevated liver enzymes.

The patient was treated with adequate fluid replacement therapy due to mild hypovolemia upon admission. Minocycline was administered intravenously, as Rickettsia tsutsugamushi was considered a differential diagnosis. However, treatment with minocycline was discontinued after the definitive diagnosis of SFTS infection.

Outcome:
The platelet count became markedly elevated during hospitalization, and serum transaminases returned to almost normal levels. The patient's anorexia improved, and she was discharged after 13 days without experiencing any fever during her hospitalization.

When the patient arrived, she had a mild headache and swelling in her left groin area. She did not have a rash, redness, or itchy bumps. Blood tests showed low platelet and white blood cell counts. They also showed unusual white blood cells and high liver enzyme levels.

Treatment:
The patient received fluids through an IV because she had a slightly low blood volume when she arrived. She was given minocycline through an IV because doctors thought she might have a different infection called Rickettsia tsutsugamushi. However, doctors stopped the minocycline after they confirmed she had SFTS.

Outcome:
The patient's platelet count went up a lot while she was in the hospital. Her liver enzyme levels went back to almost normal. Her loss of appetite got better. She left the hospital after 13 days. She did not have a fever during her entire hospital stay.

On day 5 of illness, the patient developed non-productive cough and sore throat for which a chest X-ray was done, and no abnormality was found.

The patient is advised to follow up with his primary care physician or Infectious Disease specialist as per hospital discharge instructions. He was provided with discharge medication instructions and advised to continue self-monitoring for any new symptoms or warning signs.

Hospital Course:

The 59-year-old male patient was admitted for an endovascular procedure due to recoarctation and the presence of a large pseudoaneurysm, diagnosed during routine CT scan imaging. The patient had previously undergone an aortic coarctation operation and an aortic root replacement with a biological aortic valve. An ultra-stiff guidewire was used for careful stent-graft placement, and a second tapered thoracic stent graft was urgently implanted in the aortic arch to cover the LSA origin, following acute worsening of the patient's condition during the dilation of aortic stenosis with a remodeling balloon catheter. Subsequent angiograms showed no contrast media extravasation or endoleak.

The patient experienced an acute worsening tachycardia, hypotension, and hypoxia, requiring emergency intubation and rapid fluid administration. After stabilization and extravasation drainage, a new CT angiography confirmed the sealing of the aortic rapture without contrast media extravasation and no evidence of any endoleak. The patient exhibited no neurological or other complications and was dismissed from the hospital 10 days after the operation in good shape.

Diagnosis:

Recoarctation and a pseudoaneurysm

Procedure:

Endovascular procedure with stent graft placement and dilation of aortic stenosis with a remodeling balloon catheter.

Hospital Outcome:

The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit and later discharged home in stable condition after extensive recovery treatment and close observation.

Hospital Course:

The 59-year-old male patient was admitted for a procedure to fix a narrowed blood vessel and a bulge in the artery wall. These problems were found during a routine scan. The patient had surgery before to fix a narrowed blood vessel and replace part of the main artery with a biological valve. Doctors used a thin, flexible wire to carefully place a tube covered with fabric inside the blood vessel. They also placed a second tube to cover the area where a blood vessel branches off. This was needed because the patient's condition got worse during a procedure to widen a narrowed heart valve. Later scans showed no blood was leaking out of the blood vessel or around the fabric tubes.

The patient's heart rate suddenly became very fast and blood pressure dropped very low. Oxygen levels also dropped. The patient needed a breathing tube and fluids given quickly to stabilize. After this, doctors drained fluid and did another scan. The scan showed the blood vessel had sealed and no blood was leaking. There was also no leaking around the fabric tubes. The patient had no brain or other problems. The patient left the hospital 10 days after the procedure in good health.

Diagnosis:

Narrowed blood vessel and a bulge in the artery wall

Procedure:

Procedure to fix the narrowed blood vessel using fabric-covered tubes placed inside the blood vessel. A balloon was also used to widen a narrowed heart valve.

Hospital Outcome:

The patient was moved to the intensive care unit. The patient later went home in stable condition after receiving recovery treatment and being watched closely.<|im_end|>
